Kong's New York energy with its glossy skyscrapers and fast-paced crowds. Perfect for travelers 
who want a modern upscale scene. Next up, Mong Kok. This is best for foodies, night markets,

is home to lively night markets, sneakers street, cheap eats on every corner, and neon lit 
everything. It's more local, more crowded, and more colorful with tons of budget lodging 
around. So, if you're on a tight budget and love exploring night markets and street food, 
Mongkok will be your playground. And finally, Causeway Bay. This is best for shoppers and 
youthful energy. Think of it as Hong Kong's shopping paradise. Malls everywhere, bright 
billboards, busy crosswalks, and non-stop buzz. It's young, trendy, and a bit chaotic like Hong 
Kong's version of Seoul's Myeongdong with cool cafes on every corner and endless shops to

first time in the city, these four areas offer the best mix of everything Hong Kong is known

you're here for skyline views, epic street food, or theme parks, Hong Kong has something for 
everyone. So, let's dive in to the top 10 most popular things to do in Hong Kong, including their 
price tags. and stick around until number one because this is something you absolutely cannot 
miss. Let's start the countdown off at number 10. No Hong Kong trip is complete without a dose of 
Disney magic. Hong Kong Disneyland may be smaller than its global siblings, but that's exactly what 
makes it charming. No rushing through giant parks, no getting lost for half a day, just pure easy 
magical fun. Tickets usually go for around $80 to $100 depending on the season. And the rides are 
perfect for all ages, from adrenaline junkies to toddlers and everyone in between. You can also eat 
themed snacks, catch character meet and greets, shop for limited edition Hong Kong merch, and stay 
till the finale fireworks light up the sky. It's wholesome, heartwarming, and pure nostalgia. 
Whether you're 5 years old or a grown adult, it still hits hard when Mickey waves at you. 
Number nine, part aquarium, part theme park, and part giant zoo. Ocean Park Hong Kong 
is basically three adventures in one. From adorable pandas and playful sea lions to massive 
jellyfish and polar animals, it's like jumping between continents in a single day. Tickets cost 
$60 per head. And if you're visiting with a group, you can score discounts for three people or more 
using our discount link in the description. But here's what makes Ocean Park extra exciting. After 
riding that cliffside cable car with sweeping sea views, you're dropped off straight into a world 
of adrenaline with roller coasters that literally look like it's hanging off a cliff and a tower 
that launches you above the coastline for a view of the entire park. One minute you're saying hi 
to the cute penguins and the next minute you're clinging onto your seat and questioning your 
life's decisions. Number eight. If you're into jaw-dropping, literal head in the clouds moment, 
hop onto the Ngong Ping 360 cable car in Lantau Island. This 25-minute aerial journey glides over 
mountains, forests, and the sea, ending at the peaceful Ngong Ping village. A standard cabin cost 
about $23 one way, and the crystal cabin with a glass floor is $27. But guys, I highly recommend 
upgrading to the Crystal Plus cabin at $31. It has full 360° glass walls and a glass floor. So, 
yep, you'll be floating and seeing exactly how high you are. It's kind of scary, kind of funny, 
but honestly, we're so glad we booked this one. And here's a little budget hack. Book the 
standard cabin one way and the crystal plus cabin on the way back. You still get 
that crystal clear gondola experience, but you'll save money on half the ride. Once you 
reach the top, visit the Tian Tan Big Buddha, one of the largest outdoor seated Buddhas in the 
world, and climb up the steps for a truly majestic view. Then walk over to the Po Lin Monastery 
for some temple food, explore the village shops, and just soak in the slower mountain vibes. 
It's the perfect escape from the busy city. Number seven. If you're looking for the 
most iconic skyline view in Hong Kong, head straight to Victoria Peak, the city's highest 
point with crazy panoramic views of skyscrapers, Victoria Harbour, and lush mountains that make 
Hong Kong look like a futuristic jungle. The best way to get there is via the Peak Tram, a 
super steep funicular ride where the buildings look like they're tilted sideways because of how 
steep the track is. Roundtrip combo tickets for the Peak Tram plus the Sky Terrace 428 viewpoint 
go for around $19. And for an extra discount, you can use the coupon code AngelicaWandersKlook 
to save a little more. Once you reach the top, take in those 360° views. Snap those postcard 
worthy photos and stay until sunset to watch the skyline transform into a glittering light 
show. Number six, experience Hong Kong's best museums. Art lovers and history buffs, this one's 
for you. Start in West Kowloon at M+ Museum, a hub for contemporary visual culture. Think giant 
installations, digital art, Asian pop culture, and quirky photography. Tickets start at 
around 25 bucks, and exhibitions change often, so there's always something new to see. Then, 
right next door is the Hong Kong Palace Museum, where you can admire centuries old treasures from 
Beijing's forbidden city, gold antiques, intricate pottery, ancient paintings. It's like stepping 
into history without flying to mainland China. A standard entrance is around 9 bucks and some 
special exhibits may cost extra, but they're worth it if you're into royal treasures and dynasties. 
Number five, if there's one budget friendly must-do in Hong Kong, it's sailing across Victoria 
Harbour on the iconic Star Ferry. You can take a ride from TST to Central and it's super quick, 
just a 5 to 10 minute ride and costs less than a street snack. But the views unreal. You get front 
row seats to skyscrapers, mountain silhouettes, and Hong Kong's most famous landmarks on both 
sides of the harbour. For a full cinematic moment, go at sunset or catch the Symphony of Light Show, 
which runs every evening at 8:00 p.m. It only lasts about 10 minutes, but the entire harbor 
sparkles while you cruise through it. Cheap, scenic, iconic, and you can easily tap in and out 
with your octopus card. Hassle-free. Number four, walk the Avenue of Stars at Tsim Sha Tsui's 
waterfront. Picture the Hollywood Walk of Fame, but with a stunning waterfront backdrop. As you 
stroll along Tsim Sha Tsui's famous promenade, you'll get some of the best nighttime views in 
Hong Kong with neon reflections dancing on the water. Welcome to the Avenue of Stars, the 
city's tribute to its legendary film icons like Bruce Lee, Jackie Chan, Michelle Yeoh, and 
more. It's completely free, perfect for photos, a mix of sightseeing, and a relaxing golden hour 
walk, all in one stop. Number three, feast at a discounted hotel buffet. Hong Kong loves buffets, 
and we're not talking about sad salad bars. We're talking overflowing seafood mountains, sushi 
stations, roast meats, dim sum counters, and never ending desserts. And let me put you in on a little 
secret. Many luxury hotels offer discounted seats if you pre-book it online. And I'm talking 40 to 
50% off. You got the Marriott, Sheraton, Cordis, Harbour Grand, and many more. A lunch buffet 
at the Marriott normally costs $60 per person, but you can pre-book it online for only 30 bucks. 
We'll link down some of the best buffet deals in the description. And another budget tip, book 
weekday lunch slots. They're the cheapest, but they do fill up fast because Hong Kongers 
are serious about their buffets. Number two, one of our favorites, take a day trip to Macau. 
Just an hour by ferry or 30 minutes by bus, Macau is one of the easiest and coolest day trips 
you could take from Hong Kong. Known as the Las Vegas of Asia, it blends dazzling casino resorts 
with beautifully preserved Portuguese heritage. In a single afternoon, you can wander through gold 
lined luxury hotels with indoor gondolas. Then step into cobblestone streets surrounded by pastel 
buildings and centuries old UNESCO landmarks. Don't miss iconic spots like the ruins of St. 
Paul's, Senado Square, the colorful Taipa Village, and of course, you just have to try the famous 
egg tarts from Lord Stow's Bakery. Ferries and buses from Hong Kong cost around the same price, 
around 20 to $25 oneway. Whether you're visiting Macau for a day or staying overnight, the mix 
of glitzy city life, European architecture, and seriously amazing pastries makes Macau an easy 
win. And if you want an even deeper dive of Macau, including affordable luxury hotels and the 
best budget eats, we also have a full Macau travel guide with a 3-day budget itinerary on 
our YouTube channel. And now for number one, our ultimate favorite thing to do in Hong Kong 
is indulge in Hong Kong's street food scene. Because in Hong Kong, food isn't just food, it's 
culture. Here are eight classic Hong Kong eats you absolutely need to try. Number one, curry 
fish balls. The OG street snack. Imagine bouncy fish balls bathing in a rich spicy curry sauce. 
Every bite is loaded with flavor and you'll find them literally on every corner for around $2 per 
bowl. Number two, grab a freshly made egg waffle. Crispy on the outside, fluffy on the inside, and 
the plain ones cost around $3 each, but we always go for the chocolate filled ones at $4 from the 
Michelin recommended shop, Mammy Pancake. Number three, if you want something heavier and hardier, 
go for the clay pot rice. Smoky, comforting rice topped with Chinese sausage, tender chicken, 
and chewy bits of octopus. A combo we didn't expect to love this much. We got ours from a 
famous restaurant, Hing Kee, and their dishes cost around $12 each. Our favorite part is that 
toasted crispy rice right at the bottom of the pot. So crunchy and so so good. Number four, the 
pineapple bun. No pineapples here, just a golden crackly sugar crust sitting on top of a soft, 
fluffy bun resembling the shell of a pineapple. It's best when you get a thick slab of cold butter 
inside. For a classic experience, hit Kam Wah Cafe or just grab one from the night markets or local 
bakeries for around $2 to $3 each. Number five, wife cakes. This old school pastry is filled 
with a lightly sweet winter melon paste. Flaky on the outside, chewy on the inside, and not 
overly sweet. Individual pieces usually cost$1 to$2 dollars each and we always grab ours from Ki 
Tsui Cake Shop. Number six, you need to try Hong Kong roast duck or roast goose. Juicy, flavorful, 
and usually hanging proudly in front of the shop, so you know it's legit. Yat Lok is a famous 
Michelin starred spot known for its roasted goose, and you can get a rice meal for about $10. But if 
you're on a tighter budget, head to Cafe De Coral, a popular casual chain with affordable quick rice 
meals and all kinds of yummy Chinese eats. Their roast duck meal is around $7 and it even comes 
with hot tea. Number seven, for a true local breakfast experience, visit the Australia Dairy 
Company, and yes, it has Australia in the name, but trust me, it's a Hong Kong owned institution 
that's been running for over 50 years. The owner just used to work on a dairy farm in Australia. 
Expect silky scrambled eggs, buttery toast, ham macaroni soup, and iced milk tea. This 
full classic Hong Kong breakfast costs about $7 for the whole set. And number eight, last 
but definitely not the least, dim sum. The ultimate Hong Kong comfort food. Whether you love 
hakaw, xiao long bao, lava pao, veggie dumplings, or fried shrimp puffs, which is our personal 
weakness, the variety is endless. Each order usually ranges from $3 to $5 per basket. And 
just a tip, go with a partner or with a group of buddies so you can order everything and share 
them all. For affordable Michelin star dim sum, Tim Ho Wan is always a solid pick. Plus, their 
baked pork buns are to die for. But if you want some spots that the locals adore, try one dim 
sum, dim dim sum, or dim sum here. And yes, they all sound the same, but trust me, you can't 
go wrong with any of them. Hong Kong street food is bold, fun, and honestly part of the whole 
adventure. So, arrive hungry. And by the way, we also filmed a separate food vlog called 
the 15 must try cheap eats in Hong Kong, which hit over 250,000 views. So, if you want the 
exact restaurants and food stalls that we visited, including a map, we've linked that video in the 
description below so you only spend your calories on the good stuff. Well, now that you've seen the 
top 10 most popular tours and activities in Hong Kong, let's talk about the best ways to get around 
the city. Sure, you can always load your Octopus card for commuting on trains, buses, and ferries, 
or you could just take expensive Ubers from point to point all day. But here are a few other budget 
options that might make life easier. Option one, you can take the hop on hop off bus. If you want 
something super easy and flexible, the open top double-decker bus is your best friend. It runs 
across three different routes with 22 convenient bus stops, so you can explore at your own pace 
without stressing about directions. Simply hop on or off at any point on the route, and buses come 
every 45 to 60 minutes. An unlimited pass cost $72 for one day and $99 for 2 days. Option two, 
if you're planning a full day of sightseeing and you're tired of reloading your octopus card, you 
can grab an MTR tourist day pass, which gives you unlimited MTR rides for $10 a day. Just note, it 
does not include city buses, trams, or the airport express. Here are the lines that the pass covers. 
Feel free to screenshot. And lastly, option three, the most efficient way to explore Hong Kong and 
surprisingly affordable if you're traveling with a group. You can book a private car charter. You 
get your own dedicated driver, an 8-hour ride, and full control over your itinerary. And guess 
what? Fuel is already included, and prices go for around $322, good for six people. That's about $54 
per head, making it even cheaper than the hop-on hop-off bus. This is the ultimate budget hack if 
you're traveling with a group. Skip the commuting, skip the stress, and just enjoy your customized 
adventure. Well, now that we've covered the best areas to stay, the best things to do, and the 
best food to eat, it's time for us to put it all together and form the ultimate 5-day budget 
itinerary and cost breakdown. Feel free to save this video for future reference. So, let's 
talk pre-expenses. Before your trip begins, you generally booked your piso sale, your hotels, 
and your eSIMs in advance. Your pre-expenses would probably amount to around $298 per head. For 
day one, welcome to Hong Kong. After landing, activate your eSIM, grab an octopus card at the 
airport counter, and hop on either the airport bus or the airport express straight into the 
city. Once you've checked in and dropped your bags at your hotel, it's time to start strong. 
Head up to Victoria Peak via the Peak Tram and enjoy those insane 360 degree views from the 
Sky Terrace 428. Stay until golden hour for that dramatic Hong Kong postcard moment. Then 
after soaking it all in, head down to Temple Street or Mongkok for some street food dinner. 
Egg waffles, curry fish balls, clay pot rice, or street side dim sum. It's the perfect first 
night feast to kick off your Hong Kong adventure. The total estimated cost for day one is $70 per 
head. Now, day two is all about childhood magic. Hop onto the MTR and make your way to Hong Kong 
Disneyland. Spend the whole day jumping on rides, exploring themed lands, catching the parades, 
snacking on all the cute Disney treats, and staying for the nighttime fireworks. Then end 
your night with a comforting roast goose dinner at Yat Lok before heading back to your hotel. The 
total estimated cost for day two is $119 per head. Now for day three, it's theme park round two, 
but a totally different vibe. Head to Ocean Park for thrilling rides, marine life encounters, 
giant pandas, and cable cars. It's a full day of outdoor fun and rides. Then in the afternoon, 
make your way to the TST promenade. Stroll along the Avenue of Stars for gorgeous night views of 
Victoria Harbour. And if you want that classic Hong Kong moment, ride the Star Ferry from TST to 
Central. It's cheap, quick, and gives you front row skyline views. Then wrap up the night with the 
dinner at Dim Sum Here before heading back to your hotel. The total estimated cost for day three is 
$120 per head. It's day four and time to head to Lantau Island for one of Hong Kong's most peaceful 
experiences. But first, start your morning with a classic Hong Kong breakfast at Australia Dairy 
Company. Order their bestselling silky scrambled eggs, toast, and that comforting ham macaroni soup 
to fuel your day. Then after breakfast, take the MTR to Tung Chung station, which is the jump off 
point for the Ngong ping 360 cable car. And again, a great budget hack is to book the standard cabin 
on the way up and the Crystal Plus cabin on the way down. Once at the top, wander through Ngong 
ping village, climb the steps to the Tian Tan Big Buddha, and explore the serene Po lin monastery. 
Grab a healthy vegetarian meal, browse the village stalls, or simply enjoy the cool mountain 
air. Head back into the city and grab a yummy, budget friendly meal near your hotel and call it 
a night. The total estimated cost for day four is $92 per head. And lastly, day five. For your 
final day, start in West Kowloon at the M+ Museum, home to modern art and digital installations. 
Then stroll over next door to the Hong Kong Palace Museum to see beautifully curated treasures from 
China's dynasties. After that, treat yourself to a good last day lunch. You can go for some classic 
dim sum because you can never go wrong with dim sum. Or if you want one last semi- splurge, 
enjoy a luxury hotel buffet. But of course, make sure to pre-book it online for that sweet 
50% off discount. Seafood towers, sushi stations, roast meats, and endless desserts. Then spend 
the rest of your afternoon shopping. Causeway Bay or Central for fashion and luxury. Mongkok 
or Jordan for quirky snacks and bargain finds. or Dondon Donki, which is Hong Kong's version of Don 
Quijote if you miss Japan style discount shopping. And finally, when it's time to head out, grab 
your luggages from your hotel, take the airport bus or airport express back to the airport. But 
before entering departures, don't forget to refund your Octopus card at the airport arrivals hall to 
get your remaining balance and deposit back. Then board your flight and wave a little goodbye to 
Hong Kong. The total estimated cost for day 5 is $72 per head. If you add all of it together, a 
5-day 4-night Hong Kong adventure would be, drum roll please, $771 per head. This estimated cost 
already covers everything. Flights, hotel, food, transportation, activities, and entrance fees. 
Imagine it already includes major highlights like Disneyland, Ocean Park, Victoria Peak, and even 
that Crystal Plus cabin going to Ngong ping. Think of this itinerary as the ultimate yet practical 
guide for budgetarians who want to experience the very best of Hong Kong without breaking 
the bank. And if you have a few extra days, consider taking a quick side trip to Macau since 
it's just an hour away. And don't forget, we also have a detailed Macau travel guide on our YouTube 
channel if you want all the tips and tricks on how to travel Macau on a budget. On this channel, we 
teach you guys how to save money when traveling, how to book cheap flights, how to book cheap 
hotels, and just a whole lot of travel hacks. So, if this travel guide helped you in any way, please 
hit that like button, hit that subscribe button, and comment down below if you have any questions 
about Hong Kong. We do our best to reply to every single comment, so don't be afraid to ask. We 
hope you guys enjoyed this travel guide, and we hope you enjoy your trip to Hong Kong. This is 
Angelica and Aileen Wanders. Never stop wandering! [Music]

Located at the end of the tram line, Happy Valley is a welcoming neighbourhood with a slew of high-end restaurants, local cha chaan teng, and cosy cafés. Follow our guide to explore the lesser-known corners of this hillside enclave.

**Jump to a section:**

[EAT](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-the-ultimate-guide#eat) / [DRINK](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-the-ultimate-guide#drink) / [THINGS TO DO](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-the-ultimate-guide#thingstodo) / [STAY](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-the-ultimate-guide#stay)

**What is Happy Valley known for?**Happy Valley is home to one of Hong Kong’s most iconic landmarks. For decades, locals and tourists flocked to the Happy Valley Racecourse for trackside parties that offered front row views of the horse races.

**Why do the locals love it?**The area is an upscale residential neighbourhood that many celebrities and affluent families call home. Despite its leafy suburban appeal, Happy Valley is located conveniently within a 15-minute walk away from Causeway Bay.

**How do I get to Happy Valley?**There is no MTR station that takes you straight to Happy Valley. Get off at Causeway Bay station and switch to minibus 30 on Lan Fong Road. Or take the tram and get off at the terminus located opposite the racecourse.

### Where to eat

Photograph: Apple Lee

From local cha chaan teng to family-friendly staples and fine dining favourites, there is something for every taste bud in this corner of town.

Those with a penchant for the finer things in life are in for a treat. A classic Hong Kong institution, [Amigo](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/amigo), has been a stomping ground for the city’s glitterati for over four decades. To this day, the lavish French restaurant retains its old-fashioned charm with white-gloved waiters pushing around silver trolleys and guitarists serenading diners with ballads. 

Tucked away on a sleepy sidestreet, [Locanda dell’Angelo](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/locanda-dellangelo) is an under-the-radar hidden gem serving up excellent Italian cuisine. Despite its humble size, the homey restaurant delivers through-the-roof flavours with a tasting menu featuring handmade pasta, full-blood M7 Wagyu, and Te Mana lamb rack.

![]()*Cheung Hing Coffee Shop I Photograph: Apple Lee*

Then there’s the throng of local eateries. Our favourites include [Pang’s Kitchen](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/pangs-kitchen), a revered one Michelin-starred Cantonese restaurant, and [Cheung Hing Coffee Shop](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/cheung-hing-coffee-shop), offering all the classic cha chaan teng staples.  

Another must-visit is the [Wong Nai Chung Market Cooked Food Centre](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/wong-nai-chung-cooked-food-centre), where a number of nostalgic food stalls and dai pai dong reside. During the day, head to Cheong Kee for its no-frills all-day breakfast and lunch dishes, including the egg sandwich with fish cakes, satay beef noodles, and signature thick toast that goes up to 5cm in height. At night, indulge in authentic Cantonese dishes at Gi Kee, where you’ll find an array of fresh seafood and typhoon shelter-style recipes being cooked over giant woks.  
  
While there’s no shortage of bakeries in Happy Valley, [Proof](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/proof) has proven itself to be a notch above the rest. This specialty bakery uses unbleached flour and no preservatives or additives in everything they make. Keep an eye out for the small chalkboard outside the store detailing the daily specials, and head there early because things get sold out throughout the day.

[↑ Back to top](#top)

### Where to drink

Coffeelin I Photograph: Apple Lee

Not so long ago, Starbucks and Pacific Coffee were the only two options for getting your coffee fix in the Valley. But in the past few years, the neighbourhood has seen a new wave of coffee shops quietly cropping up. 

[Reaction Coffee Roasters](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/reaction-coffee-roasters) has almost 10 years of experience in coffee roasting. Looking to introduce its specialty roasts to more people, the brand expanded its coffee operations and opened up a cafe on Sing Woo Road in 2018. Complementing its range of house-roasted coffee, Reaction offers an Aussie-style brunch menu with plenty of healthy plant-based options. 

![]()*Reaction Coffee Roasters I Photograph: Apple Lee*

Another firm favourite amongst locals, [Coffeelin](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/coffeelin-happy-valley) is a retro-inspired coffee and cocktail bar with a vibrant atmosphere. The Milanese-style cafe sources its beans from Italian roasting company Torrefazione Griso and offers signature drinks such as Misty Forest (espresso with cocoa husk tea).

[↑ Back to top](#top)

Advertising

### Things to do and places to see

Happy Valley Racecourse I Photograph: Apple Lee

Your visit to Happy Valley isn’t complete without a stop at the [racecourse](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-racecourse). The racing season usually runs from September to July, and the track is always filled with eager spectators and party-goers when the races take place on Wednesday nights. When there aren’t races going on, many locals enjoy running or walking around the track to admire the stunning views of the surrounding skyline.

![]()*Tung Lin Kok Yuen I Photograph: Shutterstock*

For history buffs, you can visit [Tung Lin Kok Yuen](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/attractions/tung-lin-kok-yuen), a historic Buddhist monastery and nunnery founded in 1935 by Lady Clara Ho Tung. Situated near the top of Shan Kwong Road, the landmark is declared a monument in 2017. The building structures feature a unique mix of traditional Chinese designs, such as flying eaves and glazed tile roofs, and European influences like stained-glass windows.

![]()*Shh Massage & Spa miI Photograph: Apple Lee*

There are a surprising large number of spas and massage spots located around the Valley. [Ten Feet Tall](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/health-and-beauty/ten-feet-tall) offers a relaxing reflexology experience in an escapist tropical-inspired setting, while the new kid on the block, [Shh Massage & Spa](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/health-and-beauty/shh-massage-spa), is perfect for when you’re in need of some serious pampering, offering everything from acupressure and hot stone massage to lymphatic drainage and meridian therapy.

[↑ Back to top](#top)

### Where to stay

Photograph: Courtesy Dorsett Wanchai

There are a lot of hotels located in the Causeway Bay and Wan Chai area that is perfect for business travellers or tourists, but the closest one is \*[Dorsett Wanchai, Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/hotels/dorsett-wanchai-hong-kong), a four-star hotel located adjacent to the horse riding ground. Its convenient location allows visitors easy access to shopping and dining destinations in the area. 

*\*Dorsett Wanchai, Hong Kong is currently serving as a* [*quarantine hotel*](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/travel/the-best-quarantine-hotels-in-hong-kong) *and only accepting travellers entering the city from now until October 31, 2022.*

[↑ Back to top](#top)

This slowly gentrifying neighbourhood is a surprisingly photogenic area and is home to one of the best places to Instagram in Hong Kong.  Start here to discover the best of what this waterside neighborhood has to offer.

# Hong Kong travel guide for food lovers

## Introduction to Hong Kong

In this Hong Kong Travel Guide for Food Lovers you’ll find personal tips and recommendations about where to stay, things to do, and details about some of the best food to eat in Hong Kong.

Home to one of the world’s most spectacular skylines with a natural harbor and lush green mountains, Hong Kong is a densely packed city where you’ll discover fresh wet markets slotted between skyscrapers and delicious food nearly everywhere you look.

From sightseeing to shopping to eating, Hong Kong offers you a full sensory travel experience.

Let’s jump right into this Hong Kong food and travel guide!

## Download this guide as an eBook!

You’ll be able to access this entire Hong Kong guide offline and have it at the tips of your fingers on a computer, mobile phone or tablet. 2 ways to download:

![Hong Kong food guide](https://migrationology.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/hong-kong-travel-food-guide.jpg)

![Hong Kong food guide](https://migrationology.com/wp-content/uploads/2016/11/hong-kong-travel-food-guide.jpg)

### 1. Amazon Kindle

[Click here to buy on Amazon Kindle](http://amzn.to/2iqdF4o).

### 2. Free PDF (limited time)

[Click here to download free PDF](https://goo.gl/ELgoJ6) (right now it’s *free for a limited time*, so download now!). However, if you’ve benefitted from this guide, please consider [making a quick donation](https://www.paypal.com/cgi-bin/webscr?cmd=_s-xclick&hosted_button_id=NYU3W9LAEUM2U) (it costs $4.99 on Amazon).

![Thai Airways](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-gWCgDZS/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-293-X3.jpg)

## Arriving and leaving Hong Kong

[Hong Kong International Airport](http://www.hongkongairport.com/eng/index.html) (HKIA) is the main airport gateway to Hong Kong, and one of the busiest airports in the world in terms of both passengers and cargo.

The airport is modern and clean, relatively spacious with a number of different terminals, and I think they’ve done a pretty good job (similar to [Seoul Incheon](https://migrationology.com/travel-guides/seoul-korea/)) in handling passengers efficiently (although there can be long lines at times).

HKIA is located on its own island about 34 km from the center of Hong Kong island, and depending on the mode of transportation you take and the destination you’re going in Hong Kong, it can take around 30 mins to 1 hour to get to the center of the city.

### Getting from the airport to Hong Kong Central

When you have your hotel or destination in Hong Kong ready, then you can decide the best way to get from the airport to Hong Kong. In all cases, it’s relative easy and straight forward and the great thing about Hong Kong is that signs are always written in English. Here are a few options you have for transportation.

![Terminal at Hong Kong International Airport](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-fTCdzzg/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-292-X3.jpg)

### Hong Kong visa

Unlike the mainland of the People’s Republic of China, the Government of Hong Kong offers a pretty liberal visa policy for many countries around the world.

You will need to do your own individual research depending on what country you’re from, but there’s a pretty extensive list of countries that are able to get 90 days on arrival for free (or 30 or 14 days for free) without applying for a Hong Kong visa. Wikipedia has a very complete guide about [Hong Kong visas](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Visa_policy_of_Hong_Kong).

I’m a [US citizen](https://travel.state.gov/content/passports/en/country/hongkong.html), and so long as your passport is valid for over one month after date of intended stay, you simply land in Hong Kong and get stamped in at immigration for 90 days. I’ve been to Hong Kong three or four times and never had any issues.

![Places to stay in Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-QBHsHBN/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-289-X3.jpg)

## Places to stay in Hong Kong

Just like in all my [travel guides for food lovers](https://migrationology.com/travel-guides/), I like to break down the accommodation in a city by the area or neighborhood.

Along with the actual hotel itself and your requirements, staying in a good location when you travel to any city is very important. Hotel prices in Hong Kong can be very high, similar to London or New York City, but at the same time you’ll also find some decent budget options if you search around.

For this accommodation section in this Hong Kong travel guide I’ve listed a few areas that I would recommend along with hotel suggestions for each of the areas.

### 1. Hong Kong Central

Located on Hong Kong Island, around Hong Kong station to Sheung Wan and Admiralty, this is the center of Hong Kong commerce and business. If you want to be on Hong Kong Island, in the middle of it all, this is the best area to find a hotel. There are limitless options for food, shopping, and wandering. For a luxury option, on our latest trip to Hong Kong my wife and I were sponsored to stay at [JW Marriott Hotel Hong Kong](https://www.agoda.com/jw-marriott-hotel-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029), and we had an amazing stay. There aren’t many budget options around Hong Kong Central, but there are some mid-ranged hotels like [338 Apartment](https://www.agoda.com/338-apartment/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029).

### 2. Causeway Bay

To still be on Hong Kong Island, but a little further away from the main central banking district of Hong Kong Central and in an area of Hong Kong that has a little more of a neighborhood feel to it, Causeway Bay is a fantastic area for you to stay. I love the restaurants and eating options around Causeway Bay and the local markets. There’s a mix of accommodation available, especially mid-range hotels like [Holiday Inn Express](https://www.agoda.com/holiday-inn-express-causeway-bay-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029). On one of my previous visits to Hong Kong I stayed at [Rosedale Hotel](https://www.agoda.com/rosedale-hotel-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029), which was a decent hotel, in a great location.

### 3. Tsim Sha Tsui

One of the most popular areas of Hong Kong to stay in, offering the widest selection of hotel and hostel options is Tsim Sha Tsui, located on the Kowloon side of Hong Kong. This crowded area of Hong Kong is home to shopping malls and markets, restaurants galore, tourist attractions, and good access to public transportation. In Tsim Sha Tsui you’ll find loads of five star hotels like the legendary [The Peninsula Hong Kong](https://www.agoda.com/the-peninsula-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029) and mid-ranged hotels like [The Salisbury YMCA of Hong Kong](https://www.agoda.com/the-salisbury-ymca-of-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029) if you can get a reservation. For budget, there are plenty of options, most notably in the Chungking Mansions, where you can get a small single room or dorm bed, plus an experience at hostels like [Comfort Guest House E](https://www.agoda.com/comfort-guest-house-e/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029).

### 4. Mong Kok

I’ll just say that Mong Kok wouldn’t be my first choice area to stay in Hong Kong, but if you’re looking for budget hotel and hostels and want to be surrounded by entertainment and shopping, Mong Kok could be a good option. There are plenty of affordable and bare-bones (yet clean) hostels and guest houses like [Gold Keep Hostel](https://www.agoda.com/gold-keep-hostel/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029) and [Kam Fu Guest House](https://www.agoda.com/kam-fu-guest-house/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029).

### Where did I stay?

On my most recent trip to Hong Kong, my wife and I were on a [Round The World Trip with Star Alliance and Marriott](https://migrationology.com/round-the-world-trip/), so we had a sponsored stay at [JW Marriott Hotel Hong Kong](https://www.agoda.com/jw-marriott-hotel-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?CID=1452029). The hotel is luxurious, with great service, and it’s in a superb location within Pacific Place with direct access to Admiralty MTR station. Highly recommended for a luxury hotel option on Hong Kong Island.

## Travel packing list

Here are a few of the things I brought with me on my most recent trip to Hong Kong.

For more details, [check out my exact travel packing list](https://migrationology.com/travel-packing-list-mark-wiens/) and you can see my camera gear and electronics on my [resources page](https://migrationology.com/travel-resources/).

![Best food in Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-mZ2txK4/0/X3/hong-kong-food-7-X3.jpg)

## Hong Kong food

In a city that’s world famous for finance and its shipping industry, food is the backbone and obsession of many Hong Kong residents.

Located in a strategic position on an island off the coast of mainland China, and with a unique history of foreign and colonial influence, Hong Kong’s food scene is diverse and always exciting.

![Hong Kong travel food guide](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-hPstvDM/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-207-X3.jpg)

Here are a couple things I’d like to point out about food in Hong Kong (and just to let you know, these are not scholarly researched points, but just my personal observations from eating in Hong Kong).

In this Hong Kong Travel Guide for Food Lovers I’m going to share with you some Hong Kong street food tips and dishes to eat, but if you’re looking for a full list of amazing restaurants, I’ve included them in a separate [Hong Kong food guide](https://migrationology.com/hong-kong-food-guide/).

![Hong Kong street food](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-G35FzK8/0/X3/hong-kong-food-1-X3.jpg)

### Dai pai dong – Hong Kong street food

Literally translated to a “*restaurant with a big license plate*,” a dai pai dong is at the core of Hong Kong street food eating.

Situated on the streets and slotted within narrow alleys, dai pai dongs are street food stalls where you’ll find made to order Cantonese dishes, bowls of pork chop instant noodles, and milk tea with Hong Kong French toast.

Unfortunately, the sad thing about dai pai dongs is that with the modernization of Hong Kong, the government no longer issues new licenses for these special street food stalls. The last remaining food stalls can only be passed down in the family, and when a generation doesn’t want to take-over, they simply disappear from the food scene.

As a street food lover, I absolutely love both the food and especially the dining ambiance of eating at a dai pai dong in Hong Kong. If you travel to Hong Kong soon, don’t miss the special experience of eating at a dai pai dong.

![Hong Kong travel guide](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-8qddJm8/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-136-X3.jpg)

## 10 best restaurants in Hong Kong

Listed below are 10 of the best restaurants in Hong Kong that I’ve personally eaten at and loved for the combination of delicious food and local Hong Kong ambiance. Be sure to also check out my full list of 25 dishes to eat in Hong Kong for lots more specific food and restaurants information in Hong Kong.

Keep in mind, these are just my personal favorites, not a definite list of best restaurants in Hong Kong.

### 1. Lin Heung Teahouse (蓮香樓)

If there’s one restaurant in Hong Kong to go for dim sum, Lin Heung Teahouse (蓮香樓) should be high up on your list. You definitely don’t want to eat here for the service, but instead it’s the lack of it, that makes it such an experience. Even for the dim sum, I’ve had better (it is good though), but it’s the old fashioned, always busy, in your face, pushcart dim sum, that keeps me coming back every time I’m in Hong Kong.

**Address**: 號, 162 Wellington St, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 6 am – 11 pm daily  
**Prices**: 50 – 100 HKD per person

![Roast goose in Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-Snx5xGp/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-22-X3.jpg)

### 2. Yat Lok (一樂燒鵝)

Yat Lok (一樂燒鵝) is one of the legendary restaurants in Hong Kong for roast goose. They marinate the goose in over 20 spices and herbs, roast it using charcoal, and the result is one of the most delicious things in the world.

**Address**: Conwell House, G/F, 34-38 Stanley St, Central, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 10 am – 9 pm on Monday – Saturday and 10 am – 5:30 pm on Sunday  
**Prices**: 50 – 100 HKD per person

### 3. Wing Kee Restaurant (榮記(東成)飯店) at Bowrington Road Food Centre

After reading about Wing Kee Restaurant (榮記(東成)飯店) at Bowrington Road Food Centre on the [Stripped Pixel blog](http://www.strippedpixel.com/bowrington-road-cooked-food-centre/), I knew I needed to eat here. It’s a classic Hong Kong food dai pai dong environment, and the food is salty and oily, and absolutely delicious. Their roasted chicken is superb.

**Address**: Bowrington Market, 21 Bowrington Rd, 2nd Floor, Wan Chai, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 6 am – 2 am daily  
**Prices**: You’ll pay about 100 – 200 HKD per person

![Hong Kong roast meat](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-FgbrLC5/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-127-X3.jpg)

### 4. Yau Wun Roast Meat (有運燒味飯店)

Cantonese are experts are roasting meats and at Yau Wun Roast Meat (有運燒味飯店), located at Quarry Bay, they have an unbelievable selection of roast pork, chicken, char siu, and an incredible ginger scallion sauce to go with it.

**Address**: 985 King’s Rd, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: Lunchtime hours  
**Prices**: About 40 – 80 HKD per person

### 5. Bing Kee (炳記)

One of my favorite Hong Kong dai pai dongs for tea and snacks (and I will admit their instant noodles and fried pork is pretty tasty too) is Bing Kee (炳記). It’s a classic street food stall in Hong Kong and the service is friendly.

**Address**: 5 Shepherd Street, Tai Hang  
**Opening hours**: 7 am – 3:30 pm on Tuesday – Sunday (closed on Monday)  
**Prices**: 20 – 40 HKD

![Hong Kong travel food guide](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-Ckgc3Tr/0/X3/kau-kee-brisket-curry-1-X3.jpg)

### 6. Kau Kee Restaurant (九記牛腩)

There’s no restaurant more famous for brisket in Hong Kong than Kau Kee Restaurant (九記牛腩), and their brisket with tendon curry over noodles is well worth waiting in line to eat.

**Address**: 21 Gough St, Central, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 12:30 pm – 10:30 pm on Monday – Saturday (closed on Sunday)  
**Prices**: Around 40 HKD for a bowl

### 7. Chan Kun Kee (陳根記)

Located deep in Wo Che estate, Chan Kun Kee (陳根記) is a fantastic Hong Kong neighborhood dai pai dong. Again, thanks to [Stripped Pixel](http://www.strippedpixel.com/wo-che-estate-market-sha-tin/), I took a food mission to this restaurant and it was worth the journey. All the food was good, but their drunken chicken is exceptional.

**Address**: No.3-5, Dai Pai Dong, Wo Che Est Market,, Sha Tin, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 6 pm – 1 am daily  
**Prices**: 50 – 100 HKD per dish

![Sing Kee (盛記)](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-ktMDpCT/0/X3/sing-kee-dai-pai-dong-1-X3.jpg)

### 8. Sing Kee (盛記)

Slotted between high rise buildings right at Hong Kong Central, Sing Kee (盛記) is a Hong Kong street food stall where you can order all sorts of stir fried dishes made to order and get a real taste of a flaming wok. I love the food and the atmosphere here.

**Address**: 82 Stanley Street, Central  
**Opening hours**: 11 am – 3 pm and 5 pm – 11 pm daily  
**Prices**: 40 – 60 HKD per dish

### 9. Tung Po Restaurant (東寶小館)

Located on the second floor of the Java Road wet market, Tung Po Restaurant (東寶小館) is the loud and busy Hong Kong dining experience you want – but with pretty good service. They have all sorts of Cantonese and even Thai food on their menu.

**Address**: 2/F Java Road Municipal Services Building, 99 Java Rd, North Point, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 5:30 pm – 12:30 midnight daily  
**Prices**: Really depends on what you order, but about 100 – 200 HKD per person

![Salt and pepper abalone](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-29bCbRL/0/X3/aberdeen-fish-market-1-X3.jpg)

### 10. Aberdeen Fish Market Yee Hope Seafood Restaurant

There’s plenty of seafood available in Hong Kong, but the restaurant at Aberdeen Fish Market is a place to go if you’re looking for a fresh seafood feast. Ying and I ordered a seafood set meal that included abalone, crab, fish, and scallops. It was a meal in Hong Kong I’ll never forget.

**Address**: Aberdeen Wholesale Fish Market, 102 Shek Pai Wan Road, Aberdeen, Hong Kong  
**Opening hours**: 4 am – 4 pm daily  
**Prices**: Our full seafood meal cost 646 HKD, not cheap but amazing quality

### Also check out: [25 Dishes to Eat in Hong Kong and Where To Try Them](https://migrationology.com/hong-kong-food-guide/)

![Things to do in Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-Qq9wbTW/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-227-X3.jpg)

## Things to do in Hong Kong (Attractions)

One of the things I love most about Hong Kong is that even though it’s a huge densely packed concrete jungle, it’s surrounded by lush mountains and forests. So when it comes to attractions in Hong Kong, you’ll find things to do that are within the city like shopping and visiting temples, but there are also incredible hikes and nature activities right within or next to the city.

Here’s a list of a few of the top things to do in Hong Kong, aside from all the eating!

### Victoria Peak (The Peak Tram)

At a height of 552 m, Victoria Peak is one of the main mountains that the skyline of Hong Kong Island backs into, and it’s the place for one of the most iconic and photographed views of Hong Kong. For that reason it’s a bit of an extreme tourist attraction, but at the same time it’s well worth visiting when you’re in Hong Kong. There are two ways you can get to the top of Victoria Peak, by taking the classic [Peak Tram](http://www.thepeak.com.hk/en/5_5_6.asp), which costs 45 HKD per person (return trip ticket) and you may have to wait in line for a while, or to get some exercise and hike. I like the hiking option, which takes about 45 mins – 1 hour depending on your level of fitness, and you can burn off some of that dim sum while you’re at it. *Address*: The Peak Tram station is located on Garden Road in Hong Kong Central; *Opening hours*: 7 am – 12 midnight daily; *Entrance price*: 45 HKD for a round-trip tram ticket, free to walk.

### Man Mo Temple

Located on Hollywood Road, [Man Mo temple](http://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/see-do/culture-heritage/chinese-temples/man-mo-temple.jsp) was built in 1847, and is an example of architectural tradition in the midst of being surrounded by sky-rise buildings. You can walk around the outside and also go inside to see the giant coils of incense that continually burn. It’s not a huge temple, but a good place to stop by if you’re in the Sheung Wan area of Hong Kong. *Address*: 124-126 Hollywood Road, Sheung Wan, Hong Kong Island; *Opening hours*: 8 am – 6 pm daily; *Entrance price*: free to enter.

![Shopping in Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-9vVddDg/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-101-X3.jpg)

### Mong Kok Shopping (Ladies Market)

Located on the Kowloon Peninsula side of Hong Kong, mention the name Mong Kok and shopping is immediately what comes to mind. If you love shopping, entertainment, and people watching, exploring Mong Kok is one of the best things to do in Hong Kong. The Ladies Market, an outdoor pedestrian shopping lane within Mong Kok is one of the most well known tourist shopping markets. You’ll find everything in Mong Kok from clothes to souvenirs to electronics, and plenty of restaurants and cafes when you need a break. *Address*: Ladies Market – Tung Choi Street, Mong Kok, Kowloon, for Mong Kok take the MTR to Mong Kok station; *Opening hours*: Around 11 am – 11:30 pm daily, all businesses have their own hours.

### Temple Street Night Market

Located along Temple Street, just after it turns dark, a street full of vendors selling all sorts of random things, including knockoff gadgets, clothes, antiques, souvenirs, and even some services like massages and calligraphy, sets up along Temple Street. While Mong Kok is busy non-stop action, Temple Street Night Market has a certain calmness to it, like walking through an eerie canyon of buildings with stalls set up on both sides. It’s a nice market to walk around at night in Hong Kong, and you’ll find lots of street food style restaurants, including some famous places to eat clay pot rice (I ate at Four Seasons Clay Pot). *Address*: Temple Street, Yau Ma Tei, Kowloon – You can get there by going to MTR Yau Ma Tei station; *Opening hours*: Around 7 pm – 11 pm nightly.

![Hong Kong attractions](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-3PNVxww/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-26-X3.jpg)

### Happy Valley Racecourse

One of the things I really enjoy doing when I’m in Hong Kong is going to the [Happy Valley Racecourse](http://www.hkjc.com/home/english/index.asp) on Wednesday night to see jockeys race their horses against the backdrop of the lights of Hong Kong. Many people, both locals and expats go to hang out, have some food and drinks, make a couple bets on horses for fun, and enjoy the atmosphere as the exciting races unfold. It’s a cheap and fun attraction in Hong Kong at night. Horse racing season goes from September to July and usually races are held every Wednesday night, and sometimes on the weekends. *Address*: The Hong Kong Jockey Club Happy Valley Racecourse, Wong Nai Chung Rd, Happy Valley, Hong Kong; *Opening hours*: Races are typically held on Wednesday night during season and begin at 7:30 pm; *Entrance price*: 10 HKD entrance, and only 18+ allowed.

### Hong Kong Museum of History

If you enjoy visiting museums, Hong Kong has no shortage. But one of the best, especially for learning about the history of Hong Kong is the [Hong Kong Museum of History](http://hk.history.museum/en_US/web/mh/). I especially enjoyed the life-sized exhibitions or traditional Hong Kong culture and village life. *Address*: 100 Chatham Road South, Tsim Sha Tsui East, Kowloon; *Opening hours*: 10 am – 6 pm on Wednesday – Friday, 10 am – 7 pm on Saturday and Sunday, 10 am – 6 pm on Monday, closed on Tuesdays; *Entrance price*: Free entrance.

![Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-MSBVDbt/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-105-X3.jpg)

### Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade (A Symphony of Lights)

The downtown part of Hong Kong is located on Hong Kong Island and if you cross Victoria Harbor you’ll be in Kowloon. On the edge of Victoria Harbor in Kowloon is the famous Tsim Sha Tsui Promenade, a stretch of harbor edge where you can ingest the spectacle of the Hong Kong Island skyline and walk through the Avenue of the Stars. To capitalize on the magnificent skyline that is Hong Kong, there’s also a light show, which they’ve named [A Symphony of Lights](http://www.discoverhongkong.com/eng/see-do/highlight-attractions/harbour-view/a-symphony-of-lights.jsp). To be honest, compared to the actual skyline itself, the light show is a bit underwhelming and disappointing to me. Nevertheless, I think it’s still worth going just to get out in the evening to walk around and enjoy the atmosphere of Hong Kong at night. *Address*: Located on the harbor edge of Kowloon; *Opening hours*: show takes place at 8 pm daily; *Entrance price*: Free.

### Chungking Mansions

Home to small businesses, international restaurants, and a world of trade, the [Chungking Mansions](http://www.bbc.com/news/world-asia-24015987) is a building in Tsim Sha Tsui that just might be the most international melting pot single space on earth. You’ll discover cyber cafes, Pakistani curry restaurants, piles of cell phones, and some budget hostels all crammed into the mansion. When I visited Hong Kong on my first trip back in 2010, my good friend and [I stayed at a hostel in the Chungking Mansions](https://migrationology.com/a-night-at-hong-kongs-notorious-chungking-mansions/), and it was a travel experience I’ll never forget. Since then, the mansions have been cleaned up and safety has improved, but it remains an international destination and one of the most fascinating places listed in this Hong Kong travel guide. *Address*: 36-44 Nathan Rd, Tsim Sha Tsui, Hong Kong; *Opening hours*: 24 hours, some businesses 11 am – 9 pm or so.

![Nan Lian Garden, Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-jbx5pGx/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-91-X3.jpg)

### Chi Lin Nunnery and Nan Lian Garden

Located on the outskirts of the city, [Chi Lin Nunnery](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chi_Lin_Nunnery) is a Buddhist temple designed in a Tang dynasty architectural style. One of the most fascinating things about the temple is that it’s completely made of cypress wood and latched together without using any nails. When you visit the Chi Lin Nunnery, located in the same area is [Nan Lian Garden](http://www.nanliangarden.org/), and impressive Tang dynasty traditional garden. I enjoyed visiting the temple, but really loved walking around the garden and the neatly manicured flora and fauna. *Address*: 5 Chi Lin Dr, Sheung Yuen Leng, Hong Kong – You can take the MTR to Diamond Hill to get there; *Opening hours*: Nunnery is open from 9 am – 4.30 pm daily and the gardens is open from 6.30 am – 7 pm daily; *Entrance price*: Free.

### Ngong Ping 360 Cable Car

Located on Lantau Island, the [Ngong Ping 360](http://www.np360.com.hk/en/) is a cable car tramway that literally goes up and over mountains and dangles past harbors of water. You can take the Hong Kong MTR to Tung Chung Station and from there get on the cable car and take it all the way up the mountain to the Big Buddha and Po Lin Monastery. I’ve been on quite a few cable car rides, and so I wasn’t overly excited to go on the Ngong Ping Cable Car. However, I’m glad I went, it was one of the most enjoyable cable car rides I’ve ever been on, providing spectacular views. *Address*: You can get there from MTR Tung Chung Station, Lantau Island; *Opening hours*: 10 am – 6 pm on Monday – Friday and 9 am – 6:30 pm on Saturday and Sunday; *Entrance price*: Round trip ticket costs 255 HKD.

![public transportation in Hong Kong](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-qHxJdr9/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-blog-1-X3.jpg)

## How to get around (transportation)

In a city as dense and compact as Hong Kong, it’s a great thing the public transportation in the city is well organized, efficient, and functions pretty hassle free. Even when you see a giant crowd at the MTR station during rush hour, they literally have trains running every 30 seconds or so to meet the demand.

That being said, while it’s not extremely expensive to take private taxis, which can be a great option sometimes, getting around Hong Kong by MTR, train, or bus is very doable and affordable.

### Here are your main transportation options in Hong Kong:

The transportation system of Hong Kong is managed and maintained very well, and you won’t have a problem getting around by public transportation. Also, I’d like to mention that if you love to walk, Hong Kong is a great city for walking with sufficient sidewalks, zebra crossings that work, and plenty of hills to get your heart rate up!

![Hong Kong travel budget](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-CxMPw5t/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-254-X3.jpg)

## Prices and expenses

Hong Kong, just like [Taipei](https://migrationology.com/travel-guides/taipei-taiwan/) or [Bangkok](https://migrationology.com/travel-guides/bangkok-thailand/), are cities that can be as expensive as you want, but at the same time, there are many ways to save and keep to your budget.

According to [Business Insider](http://uk.businessinsider.com/the-19-most-expensive-cities-in-the-world-2016-6/), Hong Kong is the most expensive city to live in… in the world.

But I think that statistic is largely skewed due to the extreme high prices for renting or buying property while *living in Hong Kong*. So rightfully so, accommodation is probably the biggest expense you’ll have in Hong Kong, but food and transportation can be affordable if you want them to, and there are many free attractions in Hong Kong as well.

In this Hong Kong travel guide I’ve broken down a list of typical prices for a few things that I experienced during my trip to Hong Kong just to give you an idea of what you can expect to pay.

**Accommodation:**

**Transportation:**

**Food:**

Hong Kong is definitely not a budget destination like much of Southeast Asia, but if you can find accommodation according to your budget, or stay with a friend, your budget for traveling to Hong Kong doesn’t need to be too high.

## Hong Kong travel guide videos

During my most recent trip to Hong Kong I was there for four full days and decided to make a travel video including all restaurants, food, attractions, and everything we did during those all out days of traveling in Hong Kong. Most of the things included in this Hong Kong blog are within these videos.

(Also you can [watch the full playlist of videos on YouTube here](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Qi19xRB6UQE&index=35&list=PLeoy0zUu6bqno4U0EXvK8WuxSxkO-UiLw))

## Map of Hong Kong

I’v included just about everything, especially the restaurants and attractions listed in this Hong Kong travel guide on the map below, so you can plan our your itinerary and route more efficiently.

And here’s the [direct link to the map](https://drive.google.com/open?id=1D2qQhNFoa227ph1SWedzXV9ygTI&usp=sharing).

![Hong Kong travel guide for food lovers](https://photos.smugmug.com/Hong-Kong/i-Hjkw4pv/0/X3/hong-kong-travel-guide-food-269-X3.jpg)

## Conclusion

From the towers that make up the skyline to towers of dim sum, Hong Kong is a city where there’s always something to do and always something delicious to eat.

I hope this Hong Kong travel guide for food lovers, along with my other post, [25 dishes to eat in Hong Kong](https://migrationology.com/hong-kong-food-guide/) (and restaurants where you can eat them), will help you discover more of Hong Kong on a deeper and more delicious level.

Have a great trip to Hong Kong and enjoy the food!

Outside the racecourse you’ll find a lot more restaurants. Located a stone’s throw from the track, Amigo is a long-time fixture in Happy Valley, with some of the best French cuisine in town.

People love the old-school decor and the food at Shanghai Lane, where you can get proper Shanghainese cuisine, with a vegetarian menu available too. ![Hong Kong Cheapo logo](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/themes/cheapo_theme/assets/img/logos/hongkongcheapo/logo.svg.gzip "Hong Kong Cheapo")
![Happy Valley](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2020/07/happy-valley-racecourse_gionnixxx_iStock-1143662412-1024x600.jpg)

# Happy Valley

Looking around at the Happy Valley Racecourse, surrounded by high-end residential towers and verdant green hills, it’s hard to imagine that this was once a dank, mosquito-infested wetland where malaria flourished and claimed the lives of many who attempted to turn the area into something more. (That explains the high concentration of cemeteries in the area, in case you were wondering.)

![Hong Kong Cemetery or Happy Valley Cemetery is one of the early Christian cemeteries in Hong Kong dating to its colonial era](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2020/07/happy-valley-cemetery_font83_iStock-1072239882-770x513.jpg)

But, persistence pays off, and Happy Valley was, slowly, transformed into what it is today. Since flat land was (and still is) a scarcity on Hong Kong Island, Happy Valley was chosen as the best place to build the now-legendary racetrack, way back in the 1840s. The swamp was drained, the racetrack was built, and a town grew up all around it.

A trip to Happy Valley and its racecourse are one of those quintessential experiences while in Hong Kong, and you can use our guide to find the best places to eat, drink, and stay.

## What to see and do in Happy Valley

Whether or not you are into horse racing, you’re sure to have a blast at the ‘Happy Wednesdays’ at the [Happy Valley Racecourse](https://hongkongcheapo.com/place/happy-valley-racecourse/). General admission is just HK$10.00, and at the Beer Garden you can stand so close to the track that you can feel the earth move as the horses thunder on past. The gates open at 5:15 pm, with the first race at 7:15 pm, so best to go around race time when the excitement fills the air.

![Horse Racing Track Happy Valley](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2020/07/happy-valley-racecourse_winhorse_iStock-114380179-770x513.jpg)

Nobody on the grounds takes the races too seriously—it’s primarily a social thing, with a few fun bets placed amongst friends. But the people sitting up in the stands are not there to drink beer and bet their $2 coins. Racing is serious business in Hong Kong; according to the Hong Kong Jockey Club’s annual report for 2017–2018, customers bet an incredible HK $122 billion at the race track. Yes, that’s billion, with a ‘b’.

The best way to get to the racecourse is by enjoying another quintessential Hong Kong experience: riding the tram. One designated tram heads to Happy Valley, and you’ll see the tram’s destination on the sign at the front to know which one. The tram goes all the way around the race track, and you can get off at the terminal to find the Public Entrance gates.

![Happy Valley Tram Terminus in Hong Kong](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2020/07/happy-valley-tram-terminus_winhorse_iStock-1223452469-770x513.jpg)

If you’re really interested in learning more about races, pop into the Hong Kong Racing Museum, which is inside the racecourse. Entrance is free, and the museum is open daily from 12 noon to 7 pm, so you could easily have dinner in Happy Valley, and visit the museum before going to the races.

## What to eat and drink in Happy Valley

The racecourse itself has a number of restaurants and bars, some of which are open to non members. [Adrenaline](https://happywednesday.hkjc.com/en/venue-adrenaline.aspx) is a lounge with a fabulous open-air terrace overlooking the racetrack, and [The Gallery](https://happywednesday.hkjc.com/en/venue-the-gallery.aspx) also has excellent vantage point, but dining with a view of the racetrack doesn’t come cheap, with a price of roughly HK$390.00 and up.

Outside the racecourse you’ll find a lot more restaurants. Located a stone’s throw from the track, [Amigo](http://www.amigo.com.hk/en/index.php) is a long-time fixture in Happy Valley, with some of the best French cuisine in town.

People love the old-school decor and the food at [Shanghai Lane](https://www.facebook.com/ShanghaiLane), where you can get proper Shanghainese cuisine, with a vegetarian menu available too.

If you’re craving Hong Kong-style dumplings, head to [Dim Sum The Art of Chinese Tit Bits](http://www.dim-sum.hk) (and no, that’s not a typo), which claims to have the best dim sum in Happy Valley. They open for dinner at 6 pm, which makes it a good option for a pre-race meal.

For the hardcore cheapos, grab a cheap and cheerful meal at the Cooked Food Market of the Wong Nai Chung Municipal Services Building. Located a five-minute walk from the Tram Terminus, the food is hot and the beers are cold—what more could you need? Be sure to check out our article [‘Why You Should Try a Cooked Food Centre’](https://hongkongcheapo.com/eating-and-drinking/best-cooked-food-centres-hong-kong/) for more information.

## Where to stay in Happy Valley

Though Happy Valley isn’t the ‘easiest’ place to stay in terms of public transportation, since it doesn’t have an MTR station, it is still a good option as it’s so close to both [Wan Chai](https://hongkongcheapo.com/locations/hong-kong-island/wan-chai/) and [Causeway Bay](https://hongkongcheapo.com/locations/hong-kong-island/causeway-bay/). You could either walk, or take a bus or taxi to these two hot spots to get to the MTR. Staying in the area also gives you an excuse to take the tram more often—ding ding!

![Happy Valley in Hong Kong](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2020/07/happy-valley-street_winhorse_iStock-1221960680-770x513.jpg)

Right across from the racetrack is the [Dorsett Wanchai](https://www.agoda.com/en-ca/dorsett-wanchai-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?cid=1816036), with fabulous views of the horse races, plus a fitness centre.

Next door to the Dorsett are the [Cosmo Hotel](https://www.agoda.com/en-ca/cosmo-hotel-hong-kong/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?cid=1816036) and the newly built [Emperor Hotel](https://www.agoda.com/en-ca/the-emperor-hotel/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?cid=1816036), both with very decent prices.

The [Vela Boutique Hotel](https://www.agoda.com/en-ca/vela-boutique-hotel/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?cid=1816036) also has good views of the racecourse, and for a little extra you can book the Racecourse Room with a Balcony.

Getting a bit closer to Causeway Bay is the [Best Western Hotel](https://www.agoda.com/en-ca/best-western-hotel-causeway-bay/hotel/hong-kong-hk.html?cid=1816036), which might have slightly small rooms, but makes up for it with a wicked rooftop hot tub.

Airbnb is a good option in Happy Valley too, especially at one of the many towers which are directly facing the racetrack.

## Articles about Happy Valley

![](https://cdn.cheapoguides.com/wp-content/uploads/sites/7/2018/08/hong-kong-trams-1024x768.jpg)

### [Ding Ding! How to Ride the Hong Kong Tram](https://hongkongcheapo.com/getting-around/hong-kong-tram-ding-ding/ "Ding Ding! How to Ride the Hong Kong Tram")

A complete guide on how to ride the Hong Kong Tramways, plus what to see along the tram route.

Things to do
 Causeway Bay

Photograph: Shutterstock

What is it?With more than 100 years of history, the Tai Hang Fire Dragon Dance is a Mid-Autumn Festival tradition that commemorates the triumph over a plague that once broke out in Tai Hang in 1880.

Why go?It's quite the spectacle. Spanning 67 metres and meticulously crafted using thousands of incense sticks, the magnificent dragon is paraded through the streets of Tai Hang by hundreds of performers. In fact, it's so impressive that it was inscribed onto the third national list of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2011.

Don't miss:The Tai Hang Fire Dragon Heritage Centre, where you can learn about the craft and history of fire dragon-making. Better yet, take a look at our neighbourhood guide to Tai Hang.

Read more [...] Why go? Despite the pungent smell of stinky tofu, thanks to its mix of tofu and fermented milk, meat, and fish that’s deep fried, it’s a beloved local delicacy. It tastes better than it smells but it remains a love-it-or-hate-it affair. It’s worth giving it a try when you’re in town.

Don’t miss: Pair the dish with some sweet sauce and chilli sauce like a true Hongkonger. And if all else fails, there are plenty more street food classics you can go for, such as fishballs, siu mai, imitation shark's fin soup, and more.

# The 65 best things to do in Hong Kong 2025

Discover all the best things to see and do in the city with our ultimate Hong Kong checklist

[Facebook](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/best-things-to-do-in-hong-kong)[Twitter](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet/?text=The%2065%20best%20things%20to%20do%20in%20Hong%20Kong%202025&url=https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/best-things-to-do-in-hong-kong)[Pinterest](https://www.pinterest.co.uk/pin/create/button/?url=https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/best-things-to-do-in-hong-kong)[Email](mailto:?subject=The%2065%20best%20things%20to%20do%20in%20Hong%20Kong%202025&body=The%2065%20best%20things%20to%20do%20in%20Hong%20Kong%202025 https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/best-things-to-do-in-hong-kong.)[WhatsApp](whatsapp://send?text=The%2065%20best%20things%20to%20do%20in%20Hong%20Kong%202025%20https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/best-things-to-do-in-hong-kong)

[![Catharina Cheung](https://media.timeout.com/images/106034429/170/170/image.jpg)](https://www.timeout.com/profile/catharina-cheung)

Edited by [Catharina Cheung](https://www.timeout.com/profile/catharina-cheung)

Section Editor

Written by: [Jenny Leung](https://www.timeout.com/profile/jenny-leung)

***August 2025**: Gosh, we’ve had some really [rotten luck with the weather](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/news/typhoon-podul-t1-signal-still-in-force-black-rainstorm-to-be-lowered-at-red-warning-signal-at-11am-081425) lately, but don’t let typhoons and rainstorms put a damper on having fun in the city! The cute critters of [Chiikawa](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/chiikawa-days-exhibition) have taken over our city with a large-scale show and a [new ramen joint](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/news/japans-chiikawa-ramen-buta-is-set-to-open-in-hong-kong-this-august-070925), while K-pop icon [G-Dragon’s](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/news/g-dragons-global-uebermensch-exhibition-arrives-in-hong-kong-this-month-080625) [Übermensch media exhibition](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/news/g-dragons-global-uebermensch-exhibition-arrives-in-hong-kong-this-month-080625) is keeping fans fed after his Hong Kong concert (there’s even a [pop-up inspired by his pet cat Zoa](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/news/g-dragon-launches-zo-friends-pop-up-in-hong-kong-inspired-by-his-own-cat-073125)!). We’ll see you out there, okay?*

We don’t like to brag (much), but Hong Kong is truly one of the most unique places to visit in the world. From vibrant [street art](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/best-street-art-and-graffiti-in-hong-kong) to world-class [exhibitions](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/art-exhibitions-to-catch-this-month), classic [street food](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/hong-kong-street-food-essentials) to hot [new restaurants](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/best-new-restaurant-bar-hong-kong), [architectural feats](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/the-most-beautiful-buildings-in-hong-kong) to [secluded islands](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/hong-kongs-secret-islands) – our tightly packed city has it all.

Whether you’re visiting Hong Kong for the first time or an OG resident looking for [plans on the weekend](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/things-to-do-in-hong-kong-this-weekend), we’re here to help you experience all that the city has to offer. So here it is, our ultimate Hong Kong bucket list, personally handpicked and compiled by Time Out editors with all the things we reckon you should eat, drink, and do at least once in Hong Kong.

Since there are some pretty [amazing stuff to do in town all year round](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do), our list is constantly changing – bookmark this page so you don’t miss out!

*Done something on this list and loved it? Share it with us and tag us on Instagram [@timeouthk](https://www.instagram.com/timeouthk/) or use the hashtag #timeouthk.*

Been there, done that? Think again, my friend.
