## The best things to do in Hong Kong

[### 1. Relive the infamous Kowloon Walled City at a movie set exhibition](/hong-kong/things-to-do/guide-to-kowloon-walled-city-exhibition)

* Things to do

[Photograph: Jenny Leung](/hong-kong/things-to-do/guide-to-kowloon-walled-city-exhibition)

**What is it?** 'Kowloon Walled City: A Cinematic Journey' is a three-year movie set exhibition featuring sets and props from the award-winning Hong Kong film *Twilight of the Warriors: Walled In*.

**Why go?**The location itself is worthy enough of a visit as it's held at the original site of the actual former Kowloon Walled City. Visitors will be able to explore meticulously recreated shops and scenes from the movie, immersing themselves to see what life was like in the lost neighbourhood.

**Don't miss:**The small projection room where you can sit and gaze at the city lights as it changes from day to night and hear the roaring sound of how low-flying planes used to skim over the rooftops of the neighbourhood.

**[See the full guide to the Kowloon Walled City: A Cinematic Journey here](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/guide-to-kowloon-walled-city-exhibition)**

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/guide-to-kowloon-walled-city-exhibition)

[### 2. Eat dim sum old-school style at Lin Heung Tea House](/hong-kong/restaurants/lin-heung-tea-house)

* Sheung Wan

[Photograph: Joshua Lin](/hong-kong/restaurants/lin-heung-tea-house)

**What is it?** A historic tea house that has been in our city for nearly 100 years.

**Why go?** It's one of Hong Kong's most unique dining experiences as Lin Heung Tea House is one of the few remaining places in the city that still serve dim sum on pushcarts.

**Don't miss:**The new 24-hour branch that recently opened in Tsim Sha Tsim near Knutsford Terrace, aka the 'LKF of Kowloon', perfect for some late-night dim sum pre or post your night out.

[Read more](/hong-kong/restaurants/lin-heung-tea-house)

Advertising

[### 3. Explore an ancient underground reservoir in Sham Shui Po](/hong-kong/things-to-do/sham-shui-po-service-reservoir)

* Things to do
* Sham Shui Po

[Photograph: Cara Hung](/hong-kong/things-to-do/sham-shui-po-service-reservoir)

**What is it?**The Ex-Sham Shui Po Service Reservoir is the first circular underground reservoir and one of the oldest to exist in Hong Kong. 

**Why go?** After the reservoir was discovered in 2020, it was rated as a Grade I historical monument in Hong Kong. From the red brick arches to the granite columns, the architectural design of this space is really quite impressive.  It's open to the public for free with no signups needed.

**Don't miss:**If you want to learn more about this reservoir, join one of their guided tours by signing up on the [Water Supply Department’s website](https://www.waterconservation.gov.hk/en/ex-sspsr/index.html).

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/sham-shui-po-service-reservoir)

### 4. Experience riding the 'ding ding'

Photograph: Jenny Leung

**What is it?**Affectionately known as the 'ding-ding' (because they have bells that ring rather than a car horn), Hong Kong trams are a city icon and the method of public transport that best retains an old-school feel – where you get on at the back and pay by the driver as you exit at the front.

**Why go?**Trams are a super affordable way to tour around Hong Kong Island, where you can catch city sights from Shau Kei Wan all the way to Kennedy Town.

**Don’t miss:** The air-conditioned fleet of trams, which makes all the difference on a hot summer’s day.

Advertising

[### 5. Get drinks at Bar Leone, the number one bar in Asia](/hong-kong/bars-and-pubs/bar-leone)

* Sheung Wan
* Recommended

[Photograph: Courtesy Bar Leone](/hong-kong/bars-and-pubs/bar-leone)

**What is it?**An Italian-style neighbourhood cocktail bar founded by celebrated mixologist Lorenzo Antinori.

**Why go?**Do you really need a reason? It's literally the Best Bar in Asia! Keeping things fun and simple, the bar brings back classic cocktails but in a more laid-back style – forget the fancy stuff and just enjoy well-crafted, top-notch drinks.

**Time Out tip:**The bar opens at 5pm and there's almost always a long queue, so get there early (around 4.30pm)

[Read more](/hong-kong/bars-and-pubs/bar-leone)

[### 6. Hop on the iconic Star Ferry](/hong-kong/attractions/star-ferry)

* Attractions
* Ships and boats
* Tsim Sha Tsui
* Recommended

[Photograph: Jenny Leung](/hong-kong/attractions/star-ferry)

**What is it?** Historic transportation that takes you between Kowloon and Hong Kong Island.   
  
**Why go?** It is by far our favourite way to get across the harbour – a slamming view of the iconic Hong Kong skyline, ocean breeze in your hair, and it's super cheap. What's not to love?

**Don’t miss:** While the lower deck is closer to the water, the upper deck offers far better views. Hop on both day and night for two different but equally impressive views.

[Read more](/hong-kong/attractions/star-ferry)

[Book online](https://activities.timeout.com/s/?q=star+ferry&searchSource=3)

Advertising

[### 7. Explore an artsy junkyard at an old leather factory](/hong-kong/things-to-do/leather-factory)

* Things to do
* Peng Chau

[Photograph: Jenny Leung | Leather Factory](/hong-kong/things-to-do/leather-factory)

**What is it?**Along Wing On Street on Peng Chau island sits an out-of-use leather factory that has been transformed into an artistic space open to the public.

**Why go?**There's so much to explore inside this eccentric artsy junkyard, with graffiti, sculptures, installations, and artworks that regularly change – so you'll discover something new all the time.

**Don't miss:**Your chance to explore the rest of the island of Peng Chau! From small cafes and boutique stores to its beautiful beaches, you can easily spend a day out on this tiny island.

> View this post on Instagram
>
> [A post shared by Time Out Hong Kong (@timeouthk)](https://www.instagram.com/reel/C3wjJVGvpr0/?utm_source=ig_embed&utm_campaign=loading)

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/leather-factory)

### 8. Discover local creative talents at PMQ

Photograph: Jenny Leung

**What is it?** A former Police Married Quarters revitalised into a centre for all things creative and design-based.

**Why go?** A diverse mix of creative enterprises can be found at PMQ, where old residential units have been converted into small boutiques selling handicraft items, as well as design studios and art spaces.

**Don't miss:**Pop-ups, markets, art festivals, and festive happenings are common occurrences at PMQ, so keep an eye on their [calendar](https://www.pmq.org.hk/happenings/monthly-calendar/).

Advertising

[### 9. Feast on local cuisine at a dai pai dong](/hong-kong/restaurants/best-dai-pai-dong-hong-kong)

* Chinese

[Photograph: Shutterstock](/hong-kong/restaurants/best-dai-pai-dong-hong-kong)

**What is it?**A quintessential Hong Kong food experience, a dai pai dong is a type of no-frills, open-air eatery that's loved by locals for its cheap prices and hot-off-the-wok flavours.

**Why go?**Dai pai dongs are an increasingly rare sight due to licensing issues, so make sure you visit them while you still can. Aside from the food, dai pai dongs are all about the atmosphere. There's just something about eating out on the street with buzzing crowd noises that make this whole experience all the more unique.

**Don't miss:** [Sing Kee](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/sing-kee-1), [Ladies' Street Sik Faan](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/ladies-street-sik-faan), and [Oi Man Sang](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/oi-man-sang) are among some of our favourite places to hit up. See our full list of the [best dai pai dongs in Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/restaurants/best-dai-pai-dong-hong-kong).

[Read more](/hong-kong/restaurants/best-dai-pai-dong-hong-kong)

[### 10. Find your inner peace at Chi Lin Nunnery](/hong-kong/things-to-do/chi-lin-nunnery)

* Things to do
* Wong Tai Sin

[Photograph: Shutterstock](/hong-kong/things-to-do/chi-lin-nunnery)

**What is it?** Chi Lin Nunnery is a Tang Dynasty-style Buddhist complex located in Diamond Hill.

**Why go?**Its multiple water features – including a waterfall draped over its onsite restaurant – make it one of the most peaceful places in Hong Kong. We could spend an entire day here gazing at the lotus ponds and marvelling at the elegant wooden architecture and treasured Buddhist relics.

**Don't miss:** The adjacent [Nan Lian Garden](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/nan-lian-garden) home to an unmissable red wooden bridge and a stunning gold octagonal pavilion.

**See also:** The best [Chinese-style gardens to explore in Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/the-best-chinese-style-gardens-in-hong-kong)

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/chi-lin-nunnery)

Advertising

[### 11. Explore Hong Kong's very own salt pan island](/hong-kong/attractions/yim-tin-tsai)

* Attractions
* Sai Kung

[Photograph: Joshua Lin](/hong-kong/attractions/yim-tin-tsai)

**What is it?**Yim Tin Tsai is home to one of Hong Kong's only salt pans.

**Why go?** The island is filled with history. Aside from joining guided tours to the salt pans and learning about salt farming, you can also head to the Yim Tin Tsai Heritage Exhibition Centre where there's a modest collection of historical artefacts telling the island's unique history. 

**Don’t miss:** The numerous large-scale art installations dotted around the island from the annual [Sai Kung Hoi Arts Festival](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/sai-kung-hoi-arts-festival).

[Read more](/hong-kong/attractions/yim-tin-tsai)

### 12. Go mural hunting in Sheung Wan

Photograph: Courtesy Kyra Campbell / HKwalls

**What is it?**Thanks to street art initiatives like HKwalls, Sheung Wan is just as bustling as it is colourful with numerous murals dotted throughout the neighbourhood.

**Why go?** You'll get to see the works of artists from all across the globe within steps of each other! Murals also get worn down (sadly) easily, so it's best to visit while it still has that fresh coat of paint!

**Don't miss:** Square Street at Tai Ping Shan. This area is particularly concentrated with murals from HKwalls 2023, including a stunning piece by LA-based artist Lauren YS and a massive mural by California-based artist Jurne at Rich View Terrace.

**Why go?**The place is crawling with families of monkeys! You can find them in the trees, at nearby beaches, or simply hanging out by the road. These cheeky monkeys are adorable but try not to feed or get too close to them as they can get quite aggressive.

**Don’t miss:** Enjoy the fantastic views of New Territories along with the large number of wartime ruins that remain well preserved in the area.

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/kam-shan-country-park)

Advertising

### 41. Cycle from East to West of the New Territories

Photograph: Courtesy Hong Kong Development Bureau

**What is it?** A 60km [cycling route](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/sport-and-fitness/best-cycling-routes-hong-kong) that allows cyclists to explore different parts of the New Territories.

**Why go?** The track stretches from Tuen Mun to [Ma On Shan](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/ma-on-shan-ultimate-guide), and snakes through numerous neighbourhoods that are well worth exploring including Yuen Long, Tai Po, and Sha Tin.

**Don’t miss:** The combination of refreshing seaside views and lush greenery. Perfect for those who wish to get away from the bustling city.

[### 42. Enjoy a night of Cantonese opera at Yau Ma Tei Theatre](/hong-kong/theatre/yau-ma-tei-theatre)

* Yau Ma Tei
* Recommended

[Photograph: Freddie Rodgers](/hong-kong/theatre/yau-ma-tei-theatre)

**What is it?**The only surviving pre-war cinema in Hong Kong, the Yau Ma Tei Theatre is exclusively the home of Cantonese opera shows.

**Why go?** It’s the go-to spot to experience the traditional form of Chinese entertainment. Expect a night of falsettos, gongs, dazzling theatricals, and Cantonese culture.

**Don’t miss:** The century-old fruit market near the historic theatre where you can get your hands on the cheapest and freshest fruits.

[Read more](/hong-kong/theatre/yau-ma-tei-theatre)

Advertising

[### 43. Strike a pose with murals at Art Lane](/hong-kong/art/art-lane)

* Art
* Street art
* Sai Ying Pun
* Recommended

[Photograph: Jenny Leung](/hong-kong/art/art-lane)

**What is it?** An urban art project that gave Ki Ling Lane and Chung Ching Street a colourful facelift.

**Why go?** The numerous colourful murals make for a perfect canvas for the 'gram. 

**Don't miss:**The pastel rainbow-coloured staircase by artists Blessy Man and Henry Lau, the blue and yellow masterpiece by Rao Amandeep, and the geometric 3D mural by Hadrian Lam.

**See also:** [The best street art and graffiti in Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/best-street-art-and-graffiti-in-hong-kong).

[Read more](/hong-kong/art/art-lane)

[### 44. Rummage through vintage treasures at Select 18](/hong-kong/shopping/select-18)

* Shopping
* Vintage shops
* Sheung Wan
* Recommended

[Photograph: Calvin Sit](/hong-kong/shopping/select-18)

**What is it?**Select 18 is one of our favourite [vintage stores in Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/shopping/best-thrift-stores-in-hong-kong).

**Why go?** You can sift through a range of old tyme knick-knacks, vintage jewellery, retro cameras, stylish clothing, and many more nostalgic items.

**Don't miss:** If you’re lucky, you may even come across some thrifty designer finds.

[Read more](/hong-kong/shopping/select-18)

Advertising

[### 45. Camp out in Tap Mun](/hong-kong/attractions/tap-mun)

* Attractions
* New Territories

[Photograph: Shutterstock](/hong-kong/attractions/tap-mun)

**What is it?** Tap Mun, also known as Grass Island, is a small island that sits off the coast of Sai Kung Country Park.

**Why go?** The island is composed of rolling, grassy hillocks complete with wandering cows. It's a popular spot for both camping and kite-flying and boasts several rocky beaches.

**Don’t miss:** The village’s main restaurant, Sun Yau Kee (新有記), where you can refuel on their famous sea urchin fried rice. [Use our helpful guide to navigate around the island](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/a-guide-to-visiting-tap-mun-in-hong-kong).

[Read more](/hong-kong/attractions/tap-mun)

### 46. Visit Hong Kong's biggest outdoor carnival

Photograph: Courtesy AIA Carnival

**What is it?**The [AIA Carnival](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/aia-carnival-2023) is the city's beloved outdoor fun fest that takes place at the Central Harbourfront Event Space every year.

**Why go?**Every year, the carnival offers everything from thrill rides and game stalls to a soft cuddly array of adorable stuffed toys for lucky winners to take home. It's the perfect weekend activity with friends and family.

**Don't miss:**The entertainment! Previous years have seen circus acts and music performances take place under a big top marquee, so keep an eye out for announcements and lineups.

Advertising

### 47. Enjoy a stunning light and sound show by the waterfront

Photograph: Courtesy HKTB

**What is it?** A Symphony of Lights is a visual spectacle combining lights and music that lights up the [Hong Kong skyline](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/alternative-waterfronts-to-view-hong-kongs-skyline). 

**Why go?** Named the ‘World's Largest Permanent Light and Sound Show’ by Guinness World Records, this 10-minute light show is accompanied by orchestral music performed by the Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra. Light installations and LED displays on both sides of the harbour will make for a stunning visual treat for all spectators.

**Don’t miss:** For the best view, grab front-row seats at the Tsim Sha Tsui Harbourfront, the [Avenue of Stars](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/attractions/avenue-of-stars), and the promenade at Golden Bauhinia Square in Wan Chai, or hop on a sightseeing ferry in the Victoria Harbour.

### 48. Feel like Indiana Jones inside Lin Ma Hang caves

Photograph: Courtesy cc/flickr/ystsoi

**What is it?** An abandoned lead mine located in the Frontier Closed Area near Hong Kong's northern border.

**Why go?**Though the mine is not the easiest place to get to, it's a great destination for hikers (and photo enthusiasts) who love exploring Hong Kong.

**Don't miss:** The opportunity to feel like Indiana Jones and snap a pic inside the magnificent structure.

Advertising

[### 49. Relive the city's past at Central Market](/hong-kong/things-to-do/central-market)

* Things to do
* Central

[Photograph: Cara Hung](/hong-kong/things-to-do/central-market)

**What is it?**Built in 1939, Central Market is Hong Kong's first wet market. It was revitalised into a cultural and retail hub in 2021.

**Why go?**Dubbing itself a 'Playground for All', the market plays host to a slew of dining and shopping venues and offers a wide range of educational and cultural activities (usually for free) for the public.

**Don't miss:**All the historical details of the old Central Market that have been preserved throughout the complex, from the historical red brick walls to the hanging clock on the grand staircase.

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/central-market)

[### 50. Place your bets at Happy Wednesday](/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-racecourse)

* Things to do
* Happy Valley
* Recommended

[Photograph: Courtesy Happy Wednesday](/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-racecourse)

**What is it?**A special horse racing night that takes place on Wednesdays at the Happy Valley Racecourse.

**Why go?** Aside from racing thrills, there's also an exciting mix of live entertainment as well as food and drinks.

**Don't miss:** There are [regular themes](https://happywednesday.hkjc.com/en/parties.aspx) for these Wednesday events throughout the racing season – such as Chinese New Year, Valentine's Day, and French May – so dress for the occasion!

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/happy-valley-racecourse)

[Book online](https://www.klook.com/activity/2284/?aid=8869)

Advertising

### 51. Burn calories with a view on Dragon’s Back

Photograph: Shutterstock

**What is it?**[A popular and picturesque hike](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/sport-and-fitness/best-hikes-hong-kong) that offers stunning views of Tai Tam, Shek O, and Big Wave Bay as you walk along the mountain ridge.

**Why go?**It’s one of the simplest hikes but incredibly rewarding with panoramic views of the sun, sea, mountains, and outlying islands. 

**Don’t miss:** Cool off at Big Wave Bay or Shek O beach afterwards and enjoy a well-deserved meal from the eateries there.

[### 52. Learn about the stars at Hong Kong Space Museum](/hong-kong/museums/hong-kong-space-museum)

* Museums
* Science and technology
* Tsim Sha Tsui
* Recommended

[Photograph: Jenny Leung](/hong-kong/museums/hong-kong-space-museum)

**What is it?** The clue is in the name, the museum is all about space science and astronomy located by the Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront.

**Why go?**The egg-shaped theatre dome that makes up half of the museum has now become an iconic Hong Kong landmark. Plenty of action, gadgetry, and interactive experiences await.

**Don’t miss:**Watch documentaries or occasional movie screenings on reclining seats under the curved ceiling of the planetarium.

[Read more](/hong-kong/museums/hong-kong-space-museum)

[Book online](https://fave.co/3PxN4B5)

Advertising

[### 53. Rent a qipao at 20s](/hong-kong/shopping/20s)

* Shopping
* Clothing hire
* Sheung Wan

[Photograph: CS](/hong-kong/shopping/20s)

**What is it?**A qipao rental shop offering sizes that go up to 5XL in order to cater to all sizes and shapes.

**Why go?** You'll get to relive the glam and nostalgia of old Hong Kong while looking great in a beautiful qipao. There are styles ranging from mature and traditional to stylish and abstract.

**Don't miss:** [20s](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/shopping/20s) also offer photography services with different plans and price options so you can capture the memories of this unique experience.

[Read more](/hong-kong/shopping/20s)

[### 54. Walk your way up Pottinger Street](/hong-kong/shopping/pottinger-street)

* Shopping
* Central

**What is it?**Pottinger Street, known colloquially by locals as ‘stone slab’ street, is one of Central's most historical streets.

**Why go?**The street is always buzzing with local vendors selling a whole range of goods out of old wooden or steel kiosks.

**Don't miss:**All the insane fancy-dress costumes and seasonal products. Whether you're looking for a wicked Halloween outfit or Christmas decoration, you'll find it all along Pottinger Street.

[Read more](/hong-kong/shopping/pottinger-street)

Advertising

[### 55. Spend a day exploring Cheung Chau](/hong-kong/attractions/cheung-chau)

* Attractions
* Cheung Chau

[Photograph: Joshua Lin](/hong-kong/attractions/cheung-chau)

**What is it?** One of Hong Kong’s most popular outlying islands, Cheung Chau is famed for its annual bun-climbing festival, giant fishballs, and sweet mango mochi treats.

**Why go?**The island is highly walkable and teeming with Taoist temples, dried seafood shops, and snack stands. Artsy types will also enjoy the surprisingly high concentration of indie lifestyle stores, while families can amble or cycle along easy walking trails.

**Don’t miss:** Cheung Chau’s self-taught [candy master Louis To](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/the-sweet-art-of-sugar-blowing-the-story-of-cheung-chaus-candy-master), who uses a traditional sugar-blowing technique to create wonderfully artistic sculpture lollipops. Take a look at our [Cheung Chau neighbourhood guide](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/cheung-chau-ultimate-guide) to discover the best of the island.

[Read more](/hong-kong/attractions/cheung-chau)

### 56. Take pics with cosplayers at Ani-Com & Games Hong Kong

Photograph: Courtesy AFP/Peter Parks

**What is it?**Hong Kong's very own comic con, the Ani-Com & Games Hong Kong is an annual event (usually happens around July or August) that gathers every anime, manga, and gaming fan in town.

**Why go?**Whether you're obsessed with anime or just a casual gamer, the fun fair is packed with exciting performances, competitions, exclusive toys, the latest video games, and a whole lot more.

**Don't miss:**Lots of people will be doing cosplay, so don't miss your chance to snap pics with your favourite characters.

Advertising

### 57. Go on a Walla-Walla boat tour

Photograph: Courtesy Harbourfront Commission

**What is it?**Walla-walla boat is a type of motorboat that once carried passengers between Hong Kong Island and Kowloon. Its name came from the sound of when the motor hits the water.

**Why go?**The tour will take you through the storied typhoon shelter to see fishermen's boats up close, learn about local fishing culture, catch the Noonday Gun daily salute, and visit the Triangular Island Goddess of Tin Hau Shrine.

**Don't miss:** Other features at the [Typhoon Shelter Precinct](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/news/walla-walla-boat-tours-now-available-at-the-revitalised-typhoon-shelter-precinct-090522), including vast lawns, shading facilities, outdoor tables and chairs, alongside food kiosks and booths.

### 58. Ride the famous Central to Mid-Levels Escalator

Photograph: Shutterstock

**What is it?** The world’s longest outdoor covered escalator system, linking Central to Mid-Levels. 

**Why go?** How often can you say you’ve travelled on the world’s longest outdoor covered escalator? It’s the easiest way to get to the area’s many great bars and restaurants, and if you’re a fan of Wong Kar-wai’s movies, recreate the [scenes from *Chungking Express*](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/film/then-and-now-iconic-locations-from-wong-kar-wais-chungking-express). 

**Don’t miss:** Feel the casual vibes that Soho offers. Crowds usually spill onto the streets and the steep pedestrian steps during happy hours. Hop off at any point to join in the fun.

Advertising

[### 59. Marvel at incredible gilded statues at Ten Thousand Buddhas Monastery](/hong-kong/attractions/10-000-buddhas-monastery)

* Attractions
* Historic buildings and sites
* Sha Tin
* Recommended

[Photograph: Shutterstock](/hong-kong/attractions/10-000-buddhas-monastery)

**What is it?** Arguably Hong Kong’s most colourful and amusing Buddhist temples, the monastery is home to life-sized, gold-painted Buddha statues, each entertainingly unique.

**Why go?** There are 431 steps leading up to 10,000 Buddhas Monastery and the climb is lined with hundreds of statues in various and quirky positions and poses. Once you reach the complex, known as Man Fat Sze, you’ll be in awe by 12,000 more gilded statues.

**Don’t miss:** The beautiful pavilions and the crimson pagoda with more statues, as well as an Insta-worthy panorama of Sha Tin and its mountainous surroundings.

[Read more](/hong-kong/attractions/10-000-buddhas-monastery)

### 60. Boogie down at Bun's 2020

Photograph: Joshua Lin

**What is it?** [Bun's 2020](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/buns-2020) is a huge indoor roller skating rink spanning 20,000sq ft with a mini training area for newbies and a full-sized rink for the pros.

**Why go?**Roller skates, neon lights, disco tunes, and an 80s diner – what's not to like?

**Don't miss:**The cute neon-lit photo spots!

Advertising

### 61. Take a hike to Hong Kong's mini 'Grand Canyon'

Photograph: Courtesy cc/wikicommons/ystsoi

**What is it?** Por Lo Shan (Pineapple Mountain), also referred to as Hong Kong's mini grand canyon, is a unique rock formation in Hong Kong that resembles the famous landmark in Arizona.

**Why go?**Located to the northwest of Tuen Mun city centre, the 'canyon' has been shaped by nature over many years, creating lots of interesting ridges and dips, so it's no surprise that it's an Instagram hotspot.

**Don't miss:**Keep your eye out for the sharp and slippery edges! Por Lo Shan is a beautiful place so it's easy to get carried away taking in the views. Some slopes are steep and the rocks are sharp, so be careful.

### 62. Get inspired at The Mills

Photograph: Courtesy The Mills

**What is it?**Originally a cotton mill, this 1960s Tsuen Wan factory building was transformed into a design hub that celebrates the city’s industrial history while nurturing its creative community.

**Why go?** At The Mills, you can discover a wide variety of established and upcoming local businesses, relax at The Mills’ spacious rooftop parks, and see remnants of the complex’s past that have been lovingly preserved.

**Don't miss:**The six different murals located along Pak Tin Par Lane outside The Mills. Created in collaboration with the Hong Kong Youth Arts Foundation and six local artists, the murals calls on the revitalised hub's past, present, and future.

Advertising

### 63. Break a sweat at AME Stadium

Photograph: Courtesy AME

**What is it?**The [AME Stadium](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/things-to-do/ame-stadium) is a fitness funhouse that combines video games with physical sports.

**Why go?** You can play and experience all kinds of sports at this place, from rowing and skiing to basketball, horse riding, boxing, and more.

**Don't miss:** The giant e-skiing game, where you can experience being part of the US national skiing team.

[### 64. Do some bird-watching at Mai Po](/hong-kong/things-to-do/mai-po-nature-reserve)

* Things to do
* Yuen Long

[Photograph: Courtesy WWF](/hong-kong/things-to-do/mai-po-nature-reserve)

**What is it?**Located on the northwestern corner of Hong Kong, the Mai Po and Inner Deep Bay wetlands are one of the most important areas for biodiversity in Hong Kong.

**Why go?** Mai Po is home to a diverse array of birds and other wildlife you might not be able to see in other parts of Hong Kong – plus, the landscape and scenery there are jaw-dropping, making it perfect for nature lovers and photography enthusiasts.

**Don't miss:**Sign up for guided tours and experiences organised by the World Wide Fund for Nature (WWF), including the 'Mai Po Day & Night' programme, a one-night accommodation experience that includes catching the sunset in the Mai Po Nature Reserve, staying overnight at the revamped Peter Scott Visitor Centre, and observing various types of wildlife in Mai Po from the new bird-watching tower hide as well as a one-kilometre-long barrier-free wooden footpath.

> View this post on Instagram
>
> [A post shared by Time Out Hong Kong (@timeouthk)](https://www.instagram.com/reel/C0bYQYcNS3u/?utm_source=ig_embed&utm_campaign=loading)

[Read more](/hong-kong/things-to-do/mai-po-nature-reserve)

Advertising

### 65. Be a culture vulture during Hong Kong Arts Month

Photograph: Jenny Leung

**What is it?** Every March, the city becomes flooded with art buyers, collectors, and all-round enthusiasts as major art fairs like [Art Basel Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/art-basel-hong-kong-2024) and [Art Central](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/art-central-2024) roll into town.

**Why go?** It's the perfect chance to immerse yourself in the wealth of amazing creativity as hundreds of galleries and institutions showcase incredible artworks from Hong Kong and around the world. 

**Don’t miss:** If your appetite for art and culture is still not satisfied, go gallery-hopping and pay a visit to the [top art exhibitions](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/art/art-exhibitions-to-catch-this-month) in the city right now.

[See more things to do in Hong Kong](https://www.timeout.com/hong-kong/search#/?viewstate=list&categories=node-7091&page_number=1)

results.title: Where to Eat the Best Pineapple Buns in Hong Kong | Indulgent Eats - Dining, Recipes & Travel
results.content:
You’ll find a massive pineapple bun sandwich with both scrambled eggs and a big pat of butter at this local favorite in the charming neighborhood of Tai Hang. It sells out by 10AM on weekends so be sure to go early. While you’re there, you can also enjoy their velvety scrambled eggs with char siu and shrimp over rice.

## Chicken Egg Boy

Chicken Egg Boy Pineapple Bun Egg Waffle
Chicken Egg Boy Pineapple Bun Egg Waffle [...] Cheung Hing Coffee Shop
Cheung Hing Coffee Shop

This local favorite cha chaan teng (Hong Kong-style diner) in Happy Valley is beloved for their pineapple buns and old school vibes. To be honest, I flip flop between this and my #1 Sai Kung Bakery, since the butter here is spread more evenly across the middle of the bun so it’s easier to eat. Cheung Hing also serves sandwiches, with options that include luncheon meat and egg or satay beef (as pictured at the top of this post), so there’s more variety of savory fillings to pair with their crispy, slightly sweet and squishy buns.

🎥 Watch my visit to Cheung Hing on YouTube

## Bolo Bolo

Bolo Bolo
Bolo Bolo
Bolo Bolo Pineapple Jam + Butter
Bolo Bolo Pineapple Jam + Butter [...] Kam Wah Cafe
Kam Wah Cafe

## Kam Fung Restaurant

Kam Fung Restaurant
Kam Fung Restaurant

Down the street from My Cup of Tea is this popular old school cha chaan teng frequented by locals. While the topping is thick and crispy with nice flavor, the bolo bao here is thick and dense so you’re getting a higher ratio of bread vs. topping.

🎥 Watch my visit to Kam Fung Restaurant on YouTube

# Where to Eat the Best Pineapple Buns in Hong Kong

![Cheung Hing Coffee Shop](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Cheung Hing Coffee Shop](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Cheung-Hing-Coffee-Shop-min.jpg)

Hong Kong’s favorite bread is unequivocally the pineapple bun, or *bolo bao* 菠蘿包. This sweet Cantonese bun can be found in Chinatowns across the world (I personally grew up enjoying the versions from NYC Chinatown institution [Mei Lai Wah](https://indulgenteats.com/2018/09/20/best-nyc-restaurants-where-to-eat-new-york-city/#meilaiwah)), but the best versions are of course in its birthplace of Hong Kong. So here are all of my personal favorite places to get a bolo bao, from simple buns to modern takes stuffed with everything from char siu to matcha.

## What is a pineapple bun?

The pineapple bun or *bolo bao* is a traditional Cantonese bread that features a soft, squishy milk bread bun with a crispy topping made from sugar, butter, eggs and flour. There is no pineapple or even pineapple flavor in the bun—the name comes solely from how the crackled, golden brown topping resembles the outside of a pineapple.

![Sai Kung Bakery Pineapple Buns](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Sai Kung Bakery Pineapple Buns](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Sai-Kung-Bakery-min.jpg)

### ***Visiting Hong Kong?** Be sure to **[get your discounted Airport Express tickets through Klook](https://www.klook.com/activity/71-airport-express-hong-kong/?aid=2922)** to get into the city from the airport!*

## Best Pineapple Buns in Hong Kong

## [Pineapple Bakery](https://www.instagram.com/pineapplebakeryhk/)

![Pineapple Bakery Sheung Wan](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Pineapple Bakery Sheung Wan](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Pineapple-Bakery-Sheung-Wan-min.jpg)
![Pineapple Bakery Nitro Milk Tea with Mini Pineapple Bun](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Pineapple Bakery Nitro Milk Tea with Mini Pineapple Bun](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Pineapple-Bakery-Nitro-Milk-Tea-with-Mini-Pineapple-Bun-min.jpg)

This brand new small-batch bake shop is churning out the best freshly baked artisanal pineapple buns all made by hand by the owner with premium ingredients. The fluffy, buttery French brioche-style dough with the crispiest crackled topping has real vanilla bean flavor throughout, with a rich flavor that doesn’t even need the addition of butter (though it’s available for HK$3 more). Pair it with their silky smooth nitro milk tea that comes with a cute mini pineapple bun garnish, and don’t miss their cocktail bun with roasted coconut and pandan cream filling for another gourmet take on a classic Hong Kong bread. Be sure to [check their Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/pineapplebakeryhk/) for their most up-to-date schedule so you can get the buns fresh out of the oven.

**[🎥 Watch my Reel on Instagram at Pineapple Bakery](https://www.instagram.com/p/DMIWJ9wxnkT/)**

I love it so much I managed to convince the owner to supply us their buns for a special Filipino-meets-Hong Kong [**Chicken Tocino Pineapple Bun**](https://www.instagram.com/p/DMuyxr_xlq_/) at my restaurant [Barkada](https://maps.app.goo.gl/AjEr5PZELoVaTbW59).

## [Sai Kung Cafe & Bakery](https://maps.app.goo.gl/F6eyfWR5WHpVVwJA9)

![Sai Kung Bakery Pineapple Bun](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Sai Kung Bakery Pineapple Bun](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/Sai-Kung-Hong-Kong-Guide-Sai-Kung-Bakery-Pineapple-Bun-min.jpg)

My favorite local pineapple bun comes from this bakery in the seaside town of [Sai Kung](https://indulgenteats.com/2024/07/02/sai-kung-guide-hong-kong-best-beaches-things-to-do-restaurants-hiking-more/). They are constantly churning out freshly baked *bolo bao*, though I recommend ordering a *bolo yau*, aka a pineapple bun with butter, so you can [watch them cut your pineapple bun](https://www.instagram.com/p/C9xGXMJO6qL/) with scissors to add a big fat pat of butter for the ultimate indulgence. You get the ultimate textural contrast, as the perfectly golden brown, sugary topping is super crispy, and the bun is airy and squishy so it’s satisfying to sink your teeth into the bun that has a subtly sweet flavor on its own.

**🎥 [Watch me eat pineapple buns at Sai Kung Bakery](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e3sHsCvwJHA&t=120s)**

## [Cheung Hing Coffee Shop](https://maps.app.goo.gl/tKuB91uzQyac8x1SA)

![Cheung Hing Coffee Shop](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Cheung Hing Coffee Shop](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Cheung-Hing-Coffee-Shop-2-min.jpg)

This local favorite cha chaan teng (Hong Kong-style diner) in Happy Valley is beloved for their pineapple buns and old school vibes. To be honest, I flip flop between this and my #1 Sai Kung Bakery, since the butter here is spread more evenly across the middle of the bun so it’s easier to eat. Cheung Hing also serves sandwiches, with options that include luncheon meat and egg or satay beef (as pictured at the top of this post), so there’s more variety of savory fillings to pair with their crispy, slightly sweet and squishy buns.

**🎥 [Watch my visit to Cheung Hing on YouTube](https://youtu.be/vwzZrXo5Ywg?si=j3MeWkWSmve8tuJp)**

## [Bolo Bolo](https://maps.app.goo.gl/J15FeoViGWsUN9UP6)

![Bolo Bolo](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Bolo Bolo](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Bolo-Bolo-min.jpg)
![Bolo Bolo Pineapple Jam + Butter](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Bolo Bolo Pineapple Jam + Butter](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Bolo-Bolo-Pineapple-Jam-min.jpg)

This new shop with a kitschy bright red design serves delicious gourmet pineapple bun burgers, tucked away on Wo On Lane near Hong Kong’s Lan Kwai Fong nightlife district. The pineapple buns here are thicker and crispier than average so they hold up to the hefty, high quality toppings, like sous vide beef with satay sauce, scrambled egg and cheese, or the juicy balsamic chicken thigh with sweet braised onions and scrambled egg. You can also find a pineapple bun with actual pineapple jam and a big wide slab of butter for a decadent twist on the classic.

***[On my list of must-try foods in Central, Hong Kong](https://indulgenteats.com/2025/03/01/10-must-try-local-foods-best-restaurants-central-hong-kong-food-crawl/)***

## [Shun Hing 順興茶餐廳](https://www.openrice.com/en/hongkong/r-%E9%A0%86%E8%88%88%E8%BB%8A%E4%BB%94%E9%BA%B5%E5%A4%A7%E7%89%8C%E6%AA%94-tai-hang-hong-kong-style-food-stall-noodles-r22273)

![Where to Eat in Hong Kong - Shun Hing](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Where to Eat in Hong Kong - Shun Hing](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2018/03/Where-to-Eat-in-Hong-Kong-Shun-Hing-min.jpeg)

You’ll find a massive pineapple bun sandwich with both scrambled eggs and a big pat of butter at this local favorite in the charming neighborhood of Tai Hang. It sells out by 10AM on weekends so be sure to go early. While you’re there, you can also enjoy their velvety [scrambled eggs with char siu and shrimp over rice](https://indulgenteats.com/2018/03/22/where-to-eat-in-hong-kong-best-restaurants/#shunhing).

## [Chicken Egg Boy](https://www.instagram.com/chicken_egg_boy_hk/)

![Chicken Egg Boy Pineapple Bun Egg Waffle](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Chicken Egg Boy Pineapple Bun Egg Waffle](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Chicken-Egg-Boy-Pineapple-Bun-Egg-Waffle-min.jpg)

This shop in Sheung Wan serves a variety of Hong Kong egg waffles, but it’s the viral pineapple bun egg waffle that you need to try. People are currently lining up for over an hour at peak times to get their hands on this fusion of two Hong Kong classics, with the sugary bolo bao topping individually placed on each bubble and baked. Each piece is like a mini bolo bao, with a chewy and squishy inside and extra crispiness from the edges of the waffle. As a bonus, 100% of proceeds go to a charity dedicated to preserving Hong Kong culture and helping the elderly, so it’s definitely worth the wait. Be sure to check [their Instagram](https://www.instagram.com/chicken_egg_boy_hk/) as they update it if they sell out early.

## [Milk Cafe](https://maps.app.goo.gl/T2wazmZ4s2rTdbcG8)

![Milk Cafe Bolo Bao with Cheese](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Milk Cafe Bolo Bao with Cheese](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Milk-Cafe-Bolo-Bao-with-Cheese.jpg)
![Milk Cafe Bolo Yau](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Milk Cafe Bolo Yau](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Milk-Cafe-Bolo-Yau.jpg)

Where have cheesy pineapple buns been all my life? This modern cha chaan teng has locations [all across Hong Kong](https://maps.app.goo.gl/pLbNsMDcWn5Cvrnm8), and while I’ve only tried their [Central shop](https://maps.app.goo.gl/T2wazmZ4s2rTdbcG8) once, I will definitely be back for their squishy, fluffy bolo bao, which are served warm with your choice of classic butter or the unique combination of stretchy white cheese and gooey American-style cheese in the center. Yes, the cheese tastes very processed, but if you’re a fan of that nostalgic taste like I am, then you should definitely try this.

## [My Cup of Tea](https://maps.app.goo.gl/WmsxKjaFZ9DfVWeM9)

![My Cup of Tea Char Siu Fried Egg](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![My Cup of Tea Char Siu Fried Egg](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-My-Cup-of-Tea-Char-Siu-Fried-Egg-min.jpg)

This tiny cha chaan teng in Wan Chai is famous for their award-winning milk tea, but I primarily come for the pineapple bun sandwiches. While the bun itself is not as good as the spots listed above, they do a great job with the fillings, from creamy scrambled eggs with corned beef to char siu and vibrant orange-yolked fried eggs for salty-sweet combos.

## [Tai On Coffee & Tea Shop](https://maps.app.goo.gl/ZLEMGhPeQWjvwuKj9)

![Tai On Coffee & Tea Shop](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Tai On Coffee & Tea Shop](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2019/10/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Tai-On-Coffee-Tea-Shop-min.jpg)

If you love matcha, then you need to try this unique bolo yau in Yau Ma Tei. Tai On Coffee & Tea Shop is a revived old school cafe that’s kept its 1960s interiors, but upgraded the menu with proper espresso drinks (including next-level latte art) and unique Cantonese fusion items. Their loaded pineapple buns include this decadent version with matcha white chocolate, red bean and whipped butter. Don’t miss their lava egg tarts with a cookie crust as well.

## [Maria’s Bakery](https://maps.app.goo.gl/tXaEVgdWWdob7XFP6)

![Maria's Bakery Pineapple Bun with Butter](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Maria's Bakery Pineapple Bun with Butter](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Marias-Bakery.jpg)

I’ve added this mini chain of bakeries as an honorable mention in this list, as it’s probably the best value for a bolo yau (HK$12 at their [Central branch](https://maps.app.goo.gl/xT43JHG2cvxEMajy9)). Operating around the city since 1966, Maria’s Bakery serves an oblong pineapple bun sliced down the middle, with a massive slab of room temperature butter that’s the length of a stick of butter. The crust isn’t very crispy, but the bun has a nice flavor, with a good ratio of sugary crust to squishy bread and softened butter in every bite.

## Other Famous Pineapple Buns to Try

I’m personally not a fan of thick, bready pineapple buns as I prefer an airy, squishy bun. But I think it’s worth trying these famous spots so you can decide what you like for yourself.

![Kam Wah Cafe Pineapple Bun Inside](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Kam Wah Cafe Pineapple Bun Inside](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Kam-Wah-Cafe-1-min.jpg)

## [Kam Wah Cafe](https://maps.app.goo.gl/M7siqeaAXMzuv8ZDA)

This cha chaan teng in Prince Edward is probably the most famous of all the spots on this list. It’s located right near Hong Kong’s [Ladies Market](https://indulgenteats.com/2019/10/05/hong-kong-travel-guide-things-to-do-must-visit/#ladiesmarket), making it a destination for both locals and tourists alike. The flavor of the bun itself might actually be the best of them all, as the crispy sugary topping has a subtle hint of lemon to make it stand out from other bakeries. I personally find the bolo bao here to be too dense and bread-like for my taste though, like eating a dinner roll topped with a sugar crust, so you end up left with a chunk of bread without any butter as you reach the end (as pictured above). But you’re definitely getting a good bang for your buck since it’s more filling. I need to go back to try it stuffed with their signature pork chop.

![Kam Wah Cafe](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Kam Wah Cafe](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Kam-Wah-Cafe-2-min.jpg)

## [Kam Fung Restaurant](https://maps.app.goo.gl/sWuvyQRRV2JpvU4WA)

![Kam Fung Restaurant](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
![Kam Fung Restaurant](https://indulgenteats.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/03/Best-Pineapple-Buns-in-Hong-Kong-Kam-Fung-Restaurant-min.jpg)

Down the street from My Cup of Tea is this popular old school cha chaan teng frequented by locals. While the topping is thick and crispy with nice flavor, the bolo bao here is thick and dense so you’re getting a higher ratio of bread vs. topping.

**🎥 [Watch my visit to Kam Fung Restaurant on YouTube](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=K_b-PDzMbpA&t=586s)**

## Check out my other guides on [Things to Do in Hong Kong](https://indulgenteats.com/2019/10/05/hong-kong-travel-guide-things-to-do-must-visit/) and [Where to Eat in Hong Kong](https://indulgenteats.com/2018/03/22/where-to-eat-in-hong-kong-best-restaurants/) for more tips.

![](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAAAAACH5BAEKAAEALAAAAAABAAEAAAICTAEAOw==)
